reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper modifies the LSTM architecture so that RTRL complexity, which is usually O(n^4), becomes O(n^2) where n is the number of recurrent units. This is achieved by constraining the recurrent weights to a diagonal structure. After performing some diagnostic tests, the paper presents experimental results using the proposed eLSTM in actor-critic RL settings, specifically DMLab, ProcGen, and Atari. The paper concludes with a discussion on the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case.

Strengths:
The paper is well written and clearly structured. 

While the ideas presented in the paper are not novel per se, the application of these ideas to a modern RNN architecture in the context of RL is quite novel. 

The work is quite relevant for the RNN/RL communities as unlocking RTRL for large-scale recurrent models is a long-standing problem and RL is an important field of application for RNNs. 

The paper discusses some rarely addressed limitations about the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case as well as some other interesting points.

Weaknesses:
The main weakness of the paper is the empirical evaluation of the method. This ranges from an incrompehensible selection of tasks over a severe lack of external baselines to insufficient evaluation methods (e.g., lack of significance testing). In the following, I will elaborate in greater detail on these and other shortcomings. 

On Procgen and Atari, the paper lacks comparison to state-of-the-art methods or any other external baselines. In general, the authors seem to confuse the concepts of ablation studies and external baselines. For instance, TBPTT on the eLSTM architecture is an ablation study while TBPTT on the fully recurrent LSTM architecture is a baseline (which is missing in all experiments but should be included; see, e.g., [3] for design choices). The authors should compare to state-of-the-art baselines in all environments, not just DMLab-30. 

The paper should experimentally explore the limitations arising from the diagonal constraint on the hidden-to-hidden interactions. This limitation competes with the bias in the gradient estimate used by the companion learning algorithm and SnAp-1 for LSTM. The authors should compare these RTRL variants in experiments to justify their proposed method as opposed to existing ones. 

The core mechanism described in Eq. (5) that reduces the RTRL complexity has been proposed in [1] and should be cited properly. 

In all RL experiments, the authors report mean and standard deviation over only 3 seeds (at least for DMLab-30 and Procgen; for Atari I didn't find any info) without any significance testing. In particular, the use of the standard deviation as a measure of uncertainty in combination with a low number of seeds has been critisized as bad practice and should be replaced by interval estimates such as IQM [2]. 

The paper states that it focuses on "RTRL-based algorithms beyond diagnostic tasks." However, it limits these experiments to the realm of RL, where the importance of memory is often unclear. The authors should investigate how the architectural changes affect performance in some standard supervised tasks. 

[1] Felix A. Gers, Jürgen Schmidhuber:
Recurrent Nets that Time and Count. IJCNN (3) 2000: 189-194

[2] Rishabh Agarwal, Max Schwarzer, Pablo Samuel Castro, Aaron C. Courville, Marc G. Bellemare:
Deep Reinforcement Learning at the Edge of the Statistical Precipice. NeurIPS 2021: 29304-29320

[3] Tianwei Ni, Benjamin Eysenbach, Ruslan Salakhutdinov:
Recurrent Model-Free RL Can Be a Strong Baseline for Many POMDPs. ICML 2022: 16691-16723

Questions:
The proposed eLSTM architecture does not use any cell state activation function. This does not affect the complexity of RTRL so I wonder why this slightly off-standard decision was made. 

Line 21, 53, and 290: shouldn't the word "quadratic" be replaced by "quartic"? 

Why did the authors dedicate resources to reactive tasks where no benefit are to be expected or an RNN might not be helpful to begin with? I had the impression that resources for experiments are limited. This exacerbates the question why reactive tasks were explored. 

The paper states its focus is "to evaluate learning with untruncated gradients rather than the potential for online learning." I wonder on what basis this decision was made as online updates could lead to high sample efficiency, one of the key problems in RL. I feel the paper missed a big chance by not researching this connection of online updates and sample efficiency. Instead, it focussed on reactive tasks. 

The paper states that "both the feedforward and LSTM baselines perform similarly" in Procgen Memory mode. However, [5] shows that the feedforward baseline is outperformed by methods using memory in 4/6 environments. Moreover, the Procgen paper [4] (see appendix H) shows that LSTM or framestacking outperforms feedforward in 4/6 envirnments even in non-memory mode. 

In summary, I really like the presented method (and the way it was presented) and believe that it bears quite some potential. However, the empirical evaluation is very weak and I do not believe it can be fixed in the short rebuttal period. Therefore, publication of the paper seems premature at this point. 

[4] Karl Cobbe, Christopher Hesse, Jacob Hilton, John Schulman:
Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2020: 2048-2056

[5] Fabian Paischer, Thomas Adler, Vihang P. Patil, Angela Bitto-Nemling, Markus Holzleitner, Sebastian Lehner, Hamid Eghbal-Zadeh, Sepp Hochreiter:
History Compression via Language Models in Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2022: 17156-17185

Limitations:
The authors give some interesting discussion on the limitation of their work, in particular the multi-layer RNN case.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
### Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a modified LSTM architecture that constrains recurrent weights to a diagonal structure, reducing the time complexity of Real-Time Recurrent Learning (RTRL) from O(N⁴) to O(N²) for N recurrent units. After validating the approach on diagnostic tasks (e.g., copy task with sequences up to 1000 steps), the authors deploy the tractable RTRL algorithm—termed eLSTM—in actor-critic reinforcement learning (RL) frameworks. Experiments are conducted across DMLab-30, ProcGen, and Atari-2600 environments, with a focus on memory-intensive tasks. The paper reports competitive results on DMLab-30 memory tasks compared to IMPALA and R2D2 baselines, using fewer environmental frames (e.g., 1.2B vs. 10B). A discussion of rarely addressed limitations, particularly multi-layer RTRL complexity, concludes the work.

**Strengths:**  
The paper is exceptionally well-structured and clearly articulates its contributions. While the core idea of diagonal recurrence for complexity reduction is not entirely novel (cf. Mozer, 1989; Gers & Schmidhuber, 2000), its application to modern RNNs within RL is innovative and timely. The focus on *exact* RTRL gradients—without approximations—for eLSTM (Sec 3.1) provides a clean evaluation framework, avoiding confounds from approximation quality. The PyTorch-compatible implementation (Eqs. 11–12) is a practical strength, lowering barriers for adoption. The relevance to the RNN/RL communities is high, given RTRL’s potential for online learning and untruncated credit assignment. The discussion of multi-layer limitations (Sec 5) is particularly valuable, exposing scalability challenges (e.g., O(L²) sensitivity matrices for L layers) rarely dissected in prior work. The connection to biological plausibility (Hebbian updates for weight-specific "memory") is a nuanced addition.

**Weaknesses:**  
The primary weakness lies in the **empirical evaluation**, which suffers from three critical gaps:  
1. **Task Selection and Baselines**:  
   - Experiments are siloed within RL, ignoring supervised tasks (e.g., language modeling) where RTRL’s online capabilities could be rigorously tested. The manuscript states its focus is "to evaluate learning with untruncated gradients" (Sec 3.2), but this narrow scope omits key use cases.  
   - While DMLab-30 includes SOTA comparisons (Table 1), *ProcGen and Atari lack external baselines entirely*. For example:  
     - ProcGen/Chaser (Fig 1c,f) compares only RTRL and TBPTT on eLSTM—an ablation—without benchmarking against a standard LSTM or transformers (cf. Parisotto et al., 2020).  
     - Atari results (Fig 2, Table 2) show no SOTA comparisons (e.g., R2D2 or transformer-based agents), making claims of competitive performance unverifiable.  
   - The inclusion of reactive tasks (e.g., Atari/Gravitar, DMLab/`rooms_keys_doors_puzzle`) is puzzling. The authors concede RTRL offers no benefit here (Sec 4.3: "RTRL is effectively not helpful"), yet resources were diverted to these tasks instead of exploring online learning’s sample-efficiency potential (a noted RTRL strength).  

2. **Evaluation Methodology**:  
   - Results rely on just **3 seeds** with standard deviation (e.g., Table 1, Table 2). This is insufficient for robust conclusions, particularly given high variance in RL (e.g., RTRL watermaze M=10: 40.2 ± 5.6). The field now favors interval metrics like IQM and optimality gaps (Agarwal et al., 2021), which are absent.  
   - Significance testing is missing entirely. For instance, DMLab non-matching object scores for TBPTT/RTRL at M=100 (61.7 ± 0.1 vs. 62.2 ± 0.3) show overlapping variance, suggesting statistical parity—but no tests confirm this.  
   - The copy task (Sec 4.1) is trivialized (100% accuracy) and fails to stress-test eLSTM’s diagonal constraint. Comparisons to approximate RTRL variants (e.g., SnAp-1 for LSTM) would clarify tradeoffs between exact gradients and architectural expressivity.  

3. **Positioning vs. Prior Work**:  
   - The **core mechanism** for tractability (Eqs. 5–6) uses element-wise recurrence, echoing Gers & Schmidhuber (2000)’s forget gate. This connection is undercited.  
   - No comparison is made to existing RTRL approximations (e.g., UORO, SnAp-1) in RL tasks. For example, SnAp-1 achieves tractability via sparsity rather than architectural constraints—a tradeoff meriting empirical contrast.  
   - Claims about ProcGen memory mode (Appendix B.3: "feedforward and LSTM baselines perform similarly") contradict Cobbe et al. (2020) and Paischer et al. (2022), who show LSTMs outperform feedforward models in 4/6 ProcGen memory environments. This discrepancy needs resolution.  

**Questions:**  
1. The eLSTM omits cell state activation (Eq. 6: `c(t) = f(t) ⊙ c(t-1) + (1 - f(t)) ⊙ z(t)`), unlike standard LSTMs. Does this simplification impact stability or representational capacity?  
2. Line 21 states RTRL complexity is "quadratic and cubic" (time/space), but per-step time is O(N⁴) for general RNNs (quartic). Should "quadratic" be revised to avoid confusion?  
3. Why prioritize reactive tasks (Sec 4.3) when resources are limited, and RTRL’s benefits are marginal? Could compute be better spent on online learning experiments (e.g., M=1 updates)?  
4. Given RTRL’s suitability for online updates, was sample efficiency (e.g., frames to convergence) evaluated? The focus on "untruncated gradients over online learning" (Sec 3.2) seems a missed opportunity.  

**Limitations:**  
The authors transparently discuss limitations, especially the **multi-layer bottleneck** (Sec 5). The O(L²) growth in sensitivity matrices for L layers (vs. O(L) for BPTT) is a fundamental scalability hurdle. The restriction to one-layer eLSTM in experiments (due to multi-layer intractability) is well-justified but underscores RTRL’s impracticality for deep architectures. The paper also correctly notes that many tasks (e.g., ProcGen/Chaser) can be solved with TBPTT using large spans, reducing RTRL’s necessity. Hardware advances may further tip the balance toward TBPTT.  

**Rating:**  
4: Borderline reject. While the method and analysis are technically sound (and the multi-layer discussion is excellent), the empirical evaluation is critically deficient. The lack of SOTA baselines beyond DMLab, inadequate statistical rigor, and omission of supervised tasks undermine claims of "practical promise." These issues cannot be resolved in rebuttal alone, as they require substantial new experiments. Nevertheless, the core ideas hold value; I encourage resubmission with expanded evaluations.

title: Towards a Better Theoretical Understanding of Independent Subnetwork Training

reviews.summary:
The paper provides a theoretical analysis of the convergence properties of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST), for distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) optimization with a quadratic loss function. The analysis considers both the cases of homogeneous and heterogeneous distributed scenarios, without restrictive assumptions on the gradient estimator.

The work characterizes situations where IST converges very efficiently, and cases where it does not converge to the optimal solution but to an irreducible neighbourhood. Experimental results that validate the theory are provided in the Appendix.
reviews.strengths:
The paper provides a solid analytical treatment of the important problem of distributed optimization with reduced communication overhead by means of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST).

Compared to previous work, the analysis of the paper does not rely on the restrictive assumption of a bounded stochastic gradient norm.

The paper is well written – the exposition is clear, and the material is well structured and well presented.
reviews.weaknesses:
The work considers distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) training with a quadratic loss. As mentioned by the authors in Section 3, a simple quadratic loss function has been used in other work to analyze properties of neural networks. While this loss function can still provide interesting theoretical insights, it would be valuable to extend the analysis and the experimental results to more generally used loss functions.

Minor comments:

-- Line 17: "drives from" may be changed to "derives from".

-- Equation after line 217: it seems that the first part of the equation
"$\mathbb{E}[g^k] = \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{\mathbf{L}} x^k \pm \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{b} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \tilde{\mathbf{D} b} = $ ..." 
should be rewritten as
"$\mathbb{E}[g^k] = \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{\mathbf{L}} x^k - \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \tilde{\mathbf{D} b} = $ ...".

-- Equation after line 221: it seems that the first part of the equation
"$\mathbb{E}[x^{k+1}] = x^k - \gamma \mathbb{E}[g^k] = $ ..."
should be
"$\mathbb{E}[x^{k+1}] = \mathbb{E}[x^k] - \gamma \mathbb{E}[g^k] = $ ...".
reviews.questions: It would be valuable if the authors could consider a discussion or possible additional experiments to extend some of the results and insights presented in the paper to the case of distributed optimization with more generally used loss functions.
reviews.limitations: The work relates to distributed training of large-scale models, which generally correspond to significant power consumption and CO2 emissions. However, the IST method studied in the paper aims at allowing distributed training with reduced communication overhead, corresponding to potentially reduced power consumption.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper provides a theoretical analysis of the convergence properties of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST), for distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) optimization with a quadratic loss function. The analysis considers both the cases of homogeneous and heterogeneous distributed scenarios, without restrictive assumptions on the gradient estimator.

The work characterizes situations where IST converges very efficiently, and cases where it does not converge to the optimal solution but to an irreducible neighbourhood. Experimental results that validate the theory are provided in the Appendix.

Strengths:
The paper provides a solid analytical treatment of the important problem of distributed optimization with reduced communication overhead by means of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST).

Compared to previous work, the analysis of the paper does not rely on the restrictive assumption of a bounded stochastic gradient norm.

The paper is well written – the exposition is clear, and the material is well structured and well presented.

Weaknesses:
The work considers distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) training with a quadratic loss. As mentioned by the authors in Section 3, a simple quadratic loss function has been used in other work to analyze properties of neural networks. While this loss function can still provide interesting theoretical insights, it would be valuable to extend the analysis and the experimental results to more generally used loss functions.

Minor comments:

-- Line 17: "drives from" may be changed to "derives from".

-- Equation after line 217: it seems that the first part of the equation
"$\mathbb{E}[g^k] = \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{\mathbf{L}} x^k \pm \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{b} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \tilde{\mathbf{D} b} = $ ..." 
should be rewritten as
"$\mathbb{E}[g^k] = \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{\mathbf{L}} x^k - \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \tilde{\mathbf{D} b} = $ ...".

-- Equation after line 221: it seems that the first part of the equation
"$\mathbb{E}[x^{k+1}] = x^k - \gamma \mathbb{E}[g^k] = $ ..."
should be
"$\mathbb{E}[x^{k+1}] = \mathbb{E}[x^k] - \gamma \mathbb{E}[g^k] = $ ...".

Questions:
It would be valuable if the authors could consider a discussion or possible additional experiments to extend some of the results and insights presented in the paper to the case of distributed optimization with more generally used loss functions.

Limitations:
The work relates to distributed training of large-scale models, which generally correspond to significant power consumption and CO2 emissions. However, the IST method studied in the paper aims at allowing distributed training with reduced communication overhead, corresponding to potentially reduced power consumption.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
**Expanded Review:**

**Summary:**  
This paper offers a rigorous theoretical examination of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST), a distributed optimization paradigm that combines model and data parallelism. By focusing on a quadratic loss model (Section 3, Eq. 12), the authors dissect IST’s convergence behavior in both homogeneous and heterogeneous settings. The analysis distinguishes IST from communication-compressed methods (Section 2.1) and reveals critical phenomena, such as the existence of an irreducible bias in heterogeneous scenarios (Section 4.1). The manuscript’s strength lies in its avoidance of restrictive assumptions like bounded gradient norms (lines 129–132), which often lead to vacuous bounds in prior work. Experimental validation, though deferred to the appendix, is noted as confirming theoretical insights. While the quadratic model is analytically tractable, the authors acknowledge its limitations in capturing non-convex neural network dynamics, leaving extensions to general losses as future work.

**Strengths:**  
1. **Theoretical Rigor:** The paper provides novel convergence guarantees for IST (Theorems 1–2) under matrix smoothness assumptions (Assumption 1), advancing beyond prior analyses reliant on bounded gradients (e.g., [30,48]). The identification of settings where IST achieves linear convergence (homogeneous case, Section 3.1.1) versus stagnation in biased neighborhoods (heterogeneous case, Section 4) offers actionable insights for practitioners.  
2. **Preconditioning Insights:** The diagonal preconditioning strategy (Section 3.1.1) aligns with adaptive optimization techniques like Adam, grounding IST in established optimization theory. The heterogeneous sketch preconditioning (Section 3.1.2, Eq. 23) elegantly mitigates client-specific curvature variations, a contribution with broader implications for federated learning.  
3. **Clarity of Exposition:** The paper is exceptionally well-structured, with a logical progression from problem formulation (Section 2) to convergence analysis (Sections 3–4). The use of permutation sketches (Definition 2) and their connection to block coordinate descent (Section 3.1, Example 2) demystify IST’s mechanics.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Quadratic Model Limitation:** While quadratic losses are a common theoretical proxy (lines 146–147), the analysis does not address how IST performs on modern architectures (e.g., Transformers) trained with cross-entropy losses. The authors’ claim that “complex phenomena” in quadratics mirror practical scenarios (Section 3) is underexplored.  
2. **Experimental Scope:** The appendix experiments, while validating theory, likely focus on synthetic quadratic setups. Demonstrating IST’s efficacy on real-world benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet) would strengthen the paper’s impact.  
3. **Bias Mitigation:** The irreducible bias in heterogeneous cases (Section 4.2) is presented as inevitable, but the paper does not discuss hybrid approaches (e.g., periodic full-model synchronization) to reduce this bias—a missed opportunity.  

**Minor Comments:**  
- Line 17: Replace “drives from” with “derives from”.  
- Equation after line 217: The middle term (“±”) is extraneous. Simplify to:  
  $$\mathbb{E}[g^k] = \mathbf{L}^{-1}\nabla f(x^k) + \mathbf{L}^{-1}\mathbf{b} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\tilde{\mathbf{D}}\mathbf{b}$$  
- Equation after line 221: Correct the expectation propagation to:  
  $$\mathbb{E}[x^{k+1}] = \mathbb{E}[x^k] - \gamma \mathbb{E}[g^k]$$  

**Questions:**  
1. **Loss Generalization:** Could the analysis extend to smooth non-convex losses (e.g., cross-entropy) under the Polyak-Łojasiewicz condition, preserving convergence rates?  
2. **Practical Systems:** How does IST’s communication efficiency compare to standard model parallelism (e.g., Megatron-LM) in terms of wall-clock time and accuracy?  
3. **Stragglers:** Does the theory account for partial client participation or stragglers, common in cross-device federated learning?  

**Limitations:**  
The paper’s focus on quadratic models limits its direct applicability to real-world deep learning. However, the reduced communication overhead of IST could lower the carbon footprint of distributed training—a compelling angle for future work.  

**Impact:**  
This paper significantly advances the theoretical understanding of IST, a critical tool for scaling giant models. The convergence bounds and bias characterization will inform systems design, making the paper a strong candidate for acceptance.  

**Recommendation:**  
**Accept** (7/10). The paper’s theoretical contributions are substantial and well-executed. Addressing the quadratic limitation in future work (e.g., via a follow-up paper on cross-entropy losses) would further elevate its impact.

The work relates to distributed training of large-scale models, which generally correspond to significant power consumption and CO2 emissions. However, the IST method studied in the paper aims at allowing distributed training with reduced communication overhead, corresponding to potentially reduced power consumption.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

Summary:
The authors provided a theoretical analysis for independent subnet training and provided a convergence analysis in the case where communication compression is present.

The authors discussed the scenario when bias is not present and provided two analyses in the homogeneous and heterogeneous case, respectively before extending their theorems to the case with bias.

Strengths:
1. Sections 3 and 4 are well written and clear, which break the scenarios down in an intuitive way and presented the theorems and outlines clearly.

Weaknesses:
1. Introduction could be more straightforward and can dive straight into the main technical contributions of the work. 1. The authors mentioned that prior work on the convergence of IST focus "on overparameterized single hidden layer neural networks with ReLU activations". It is not entirely clear to me why the authors considered quadratic form and what is the tradeoff between their work and prior work in this regard. A more thorough explanation would be appreciated.

reviews.text:
### Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
The authors present a rigorous theoretical analysis of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST), a distributed optimization technique combining data and model parallelism. The study identifies fundamental differences between IST and methods using compressed communication, providing a precise characterization of its optimization performance on a quadratic model. The analysis begins with the interpolation case (no linear term), where convergence is established under specific conditions (e.g., matrix smoothness and permutation sketches), and extends to the general case with bias, revealing an irreducible neighborhood of convergence. Theorems 1 and 2 offer non-asymptotic rates for homogeneous and heterogeneous settings, highlighting the impact of gradient estimator bias and data heterogeneity. The use of quadratic models enables a tractable yet insightful exploration of phenomena relevant to neural network training, such as the role of preconditioning (Section 3.1.1) and the trade-offs between convergence speed and neighborhood size (Section 4.2). While the theoretical framework is deliberately simplified (e.g., single gradient steps, quadratic losses), it successfully isolates challenges unique to IST, such as misalignment between true gradients and sparse submodel estimators (Section 2.1).  

**Strengths:**  
1. Sections 3 and 4 are exceptionally well-structured, breaking down complex scenarios into intuitive components. For instance, Section 3 meticulously distinguishes between homogeneous and heterogeneous cases, using preconditioning (change of variables in Eq. 20) and modified sketches (Eq. 23) to enforce convergence conditions. The extension to biased estimators in Section 4 is equally lucid: the decomposition of the gradient estimator into an "optimally preconditioned gradient" and an irreducible bias term (Eq. 27) clarifies why IST converges to a neighborhood rather than the true solution. Theorems are contextualized with tangible examples (e.g., identity and permutation sketches), and the discussion of limitations (e.g., indefiniteness of \(W\) in Eq. 19) enhances rigor. The analysis of trade-offs in Theorem 2 (e.g., step-size dependence and bias-heterogeneity interplay) is particularly valuable for practitioners.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. The introduction could be streamlined to immediately foreground the paper’s contributions and motivations. While background on data/model parallelism (Lines 36–59) is necessary, the core challenges of IST (e.g., biased gradients in Eq. 10 and the inadequacy of prior assumptions like bounded gradients in Eq. 11) are not highlighted until Section 2.1. This delays clarity on the paper’s unique focus: the *optimization-level* differences between IST and communication compression. A concise preview of the quadratic model’s role (Section 3) would also strengthen motivation—e.g., noting early that quadratics capture phenomena like preconditioning effects (Section 3.1.1) while avoiding pathological assumptions. Such adjustments would help readers grasp the significance of the theoretical hurdles (e.g., non-unbiased estimators in Eq. 10) before encountering the technical setup.  

**Questions:**  
1. The authors note that prior IST convergence analyses focus on "overparameterized single hidden layer neural networks with ReLU activations" (Lines 51–52). While the quadratic model is justified as a tool for observing "complex phenomena" (Lines 145–148), the trade-offs versus prior work deserve elaboration. Specifically:  
   - **Advantages**: The quadratic model avoids restrictive assumptions (e.g., overparameterization or Lipschitz gradients) and enables exact bias characterization (e.g., Eq. 28). This reveals settings where IST converges efficiently (Section 3.1.2) or to a neighborhood (Section 4.1), which ReLU-focused analyses might overlook.  
   - **Limitations**: Although quadratics offer insights (e.g., irreducible bias in Eq. 30), they may not fully capture the dynamics of deep networks (e.g., non-convex loss landscapes). A discussion of how phenomena like permutation sketches (Definition 2) translate to practical architectures (e.g., ResNets in Line 47) would bridge theory and practice.  

**Limitations:**  
The work is theoretical, with no immediate societal implications. However, two limitations warrant note:  
1. The quadratic model, while illuminating, may not reflect nuances of deep neural network optimization (e.g., activation functions, batch normalization). The analysis also assumes idealized sketches (e.g., permutation-based), which may not align with arbitrary subnetwork strategies (e.g., unstructured pruning).  
2. Practical extensions—such as local steps, partial participation, or non-quadratic objectives—are deferred to future work (Section 5). While this is reasonable given the paper’s scope, empirical validation beyond synthetic experiments (mentioned in Line 67) would strengthen applicability.  

**Rating:**  
**6: Weak Accept**  
The paper makes a valuable theoretical contribution by dissecting IST’s convergence properties, rigorously addressing gaps in prior work (e.g., biased estimators, heterogeneity). The clarity of Sections 3–4 and the characterization of irreducible bias are significant strengths. While the introduction could more sharply motivate the problem, and the quadratic model’s generality has inherent limits, the analysis is technically sound and offers actionable insights for distributed optimization. The work meets NeurIPS standards for novelty and rigor, meriting acceptance.

reviews.summary:
Independent Subnetwork Training (IST) is a technique that divides the neural network into smaller independent subnetworks, trains them in a distributed parallel way, and aggregates the results of each independent subnetwork to update the weights of the whole model.
This paper aims to analyze the behavior of IST theoretically. Specifically, it considers a quadratic model trained by IST. It conducts convergence analysis under both homogeneous and heterogeneous scenarios and shows that IST can only converge to an irreducible neighborhood of optimal solution.
reviews.strengths: This is probably the first work providing a thorough theoretical analysis of IST.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. This paper should include a more comprehensive motivation for the theoretical study of IST. This could involve discussing the potential limitations of current IST architectures and how a theoretical analysis can guide future modifications to improve their performance. By doing so, reviewers will have a clearer understanding of the significance of the paper's findings and how they can be applied in practice.
2. The main body of this paper does not have any experimental results. The authors should include some key experiments to validate their theoretical analysis.
3. The authors should consider expanding the scope of their experiments beyond quadratic models to include other types of models that are commonly used in SOTA IST papers, e.g., ResNet, and Graph Convolutional Networks, as listed in this paper's reference. This would allow reviewers to better understand the generality of the paper's findings and how they can be applied to real-world applications.
reviews.questions:
1. How do the findings of this work be of any help to the future design of IST architecture? In addition, the authors should include more experiments on SOTA IST applications, e.g., ResNet, and Graph Convolutional Networks, to indicate the generality and significance of this paper's findings.
2. The authors should clarify the assumptions made in the permutation example in Section 3.1. Specifically, they should explain the case that n=d^2 and the use of Perm-1 sketch, while according to definition 2, perm-q refers to d=qn, which leads to n=d=1 and it’s a naïve configuration. By clarifying the assumptions made in this section, the reader can better understand the example and its implications for IST.
reviews.limitations: Future works are discussed in the conclusion section.

Summary:
Independent Subnetwork Training (IST) is a technique that divides the neural network into smaller independent subnetworks, trains them in a distributed parallel way, and aggregates the results of each independent subnetwork to update the weights of the whole model.
This paper aims to analyze the behavior of IST theoretically. Specifically, it considers a quadratic model trained by IST. It conducts convergence analysis under both homogeneous and heterogeneous scenarios and shows that IST can only converge to an irreducible neighborhood of optimal solution.

Strengths:
This is probably the first work providing a thorough theoretical analysis of IST.

Weaknesses:
1. This paper should include a more comprehensive motivation for the theoretical study of IST. This could involve discussing the potential limitations of current IST architectures and how a theoretical analysis can guide future modifications to improve their performance. By doing so, reviewers will have a clearer understanding of the significance of the paper's findings and how they can be applied in practice.
2. The main body of this paper does not have any experimental results. The authors should include some key experiments to validate their theoretical analysis.
3. The authors should consider expanding the scope of their experiments beyond quadratic models to include other types of models that are commonly used in SOTA IST papers, e.g., ResNet, and Graph Convolutional Networks, as listed in this paper's reference. This would allow reviewers to better understand the generality of the paper's findings and how they can be applied to real-world applications.

Questions:
1. How do the findings of this work be of any help to the future design of IST architecture? In addition, the authors should include more experiments on SOTA IST applications, e.g., ResNet, and Graph Convolutional Networks, to indicate the generality and significance of this paper's findings.
2. The authors should clarify the assumptions made in the permutation example in Section 3.1. Specifically, they should explain the case that n=d^2 and the use of Perm-1 sketch, while according to definition 2, perm-q refers to d=qn, which leads to n=d=1 and it’s a naïve configuration. By clarifying the assumptions made in this section, the reader can better understand the example and its implications for IST.

Limitations:
Future works are discussed in the conclusion section.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
### Expanded Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper provides a theoretical analysis of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST), a technique that partitions neural networks into smaller, independently trainable subnetworks, processes them in parallel, and aggregates updates to refine the full model. The study focuses on IST's optimization behavior for quadratic models, identifying convergence properties in homogeneous and heterogeneous settings and revealing an irreducible bias that prevents convergence to the true optimum. While novel, the work lacks empirical validation and practical connections to real-world IST applications.  

**Strengths:**  
This is likely the first comprehensive theoretical examination of IST, addressing a significant gap in distributed machine learning literature. The analysis rigorously characterizes convergence dynamics, including the critical observation that IST converges to a neighborhood of the optimal solution rather than the solution itself (Section 4). The extension to heterogeneous data (Section 3.1.2) and federated learning contexts (Section 1.1) strengthens the framework's applicability. The quadratic model (Section 3) serves as a tractable testbed for isolating IST’s optimization properties, and the identification of fundamental differences from compressed communication methods (Section 2.1) provides valuable theoretical grounding.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Motivation and Practical Relevance:** The paper inadequately motivates *why* IST’s theoretical limitations matter for real-world systems. For instance, it does not discuss how the identified irreducible bias (Section 4.1) impacts SOTA architectures like ResNets [14] or GCNs [43], which are cited as IST applications. Linking theory to practical pain points (e.g., memory constraints in large transformers) would clarify the work’s significance. The federated learning discussion (Section 1.1) briefly mentions device heterogeneity but does not tie IST’s bias to tangible issues like client drift or fairness degradation.  
2. **Lack of Empirical Validation:** All experiments are relegated to the appendix (Section 1.2), undermining reproducibility. The main text omits even minimal validation (e.g., synthetic quadratic trials) to illustrate key claims, such as the neighborhood size in Theorem 2 or the bias term \(h\) in Eq. (27). Including a subset of these results in the body would demonstrate the theory’s plausibility.  
3. **Narrow Scope:** The quadratic model (Section 3) is analytically convenient but fails to capture complexities of neural networks. The paper references IST successes in ResNets [14] and GCNs [43] yet offers no experiments or discussion on whether the theory generalizes to these architectures. Validating findings on a shallow CNN or a toy ResNet would bridge this gap and address the generality concerns raised in the conclusion (Section 5).  

**Questions:**  
1. **Practical Utility:** How can practitioners use the convergence neighborhood bounds (Theorem 2) to design better partitioning strategies? For example, could the bias term \(h\) inform subnetwork allocation in heterogeneous federated systems (Section 1.1)? Additionally, the authors should test their theory on non-quadratic models (e.g., a small ResNet on CIFAR-10) to show relevance beyond synthetic settings.  
2. **Permutation Sketch Assumptions:** Section 3.1 assumes \(n = d^2\) for Perm-1 sketches, but Definition 2 requires \(d = qn\) (e.g., \(q=1\) implies \(n=d\)). This discrepancy (e.g., \(n=d^2\) vs. \(n=d\)) needs clarification. Does the analysis hold for standard configurations like \(d \gg n\) (common in large models), or is it limited to contrived cases?  

**Limitations:**  
The conclusion (Section 5) appropriately notes the need for extensions to federated settings with partial participation and local steps. However, it overlooks limitations inherent to the quadratic simplification, such as ignoring activation functions or loss landscapes with saddle points, which are pivotal in neural network optimization.  

**Decision:**  
**Rating: 4 (Borderline Reject)**  
While the paper makes a novel theoretical contribution, its **limited scope and absence of empirical validation** significantly diminish its impact. The lack of experiments in the main body (despite space constraints) and the failure to contextualize theory within practical IST applications (e.g., ResNets/GCNs) outweigh the technical rigor. The analysis of IST’s irreducible bias is compelling, but without evidence of its manifestation in real-world settings, the work remains primarily conceptual. Strengthening the motivation and including even minimal experiments would elevate this to a strong accept.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
Independent Subnetwork Training (IST) is a technique that divides the neural network into smaller independent subnetworks, trains them in a distributed parallel way, and aggregates the results of each independent subnetwork to update the weights of the whole model.
This paper aims to analyze the behavior of IST theoretically. Specifically, it considers a quadratic model trained by IST. It conducts convergence analysis under both homogeneous and heterogeneous scenarios and shows that IST can only converge to an irreducible neighborhood of optimal solution.

Strengths:
This is probably the first work providing a thorough theoretical analysis of IST.

Weaknesses:
1. This paper should include a more comprehensive motivation for the theoretical study of IST. This could involve discussing the potential limitations of current IST architectures and how a theoretical analysis can guide future modifications to improve their performance. By doing so, reviewers will have a clearer understanding of the significance of the paper's findings and how they can be applied in practice.
2. The main body of this paper does not have any experimental results. The authors should include some key experiments to validate their theoretical analysis.
3. The authors should consider expanding the scope of their experiments beyond quadratic models to include other types of models that are commonly used in SOTA IST papers, e.g., ResNet, and Graph Convolutional Networks, as listed in this paper's reference. This would allow reviewers to better understand the generality of the paper's findings and how they can be applied to real-world applications.

Questions:
1. How do the findings of this work be of any help to the future design of IST architecture? In addition, the authors should include more experiments on SOTA IST applications, e.g., ResNet, and Graph Convolutional Networks, to indicate the generality and significance of this paper's findings.
2. The authors should clarify the assumptions made in the permutation example in Section 3.1. Specifically, they should explain the case that n=d^2 and the use of Perm-1 sketch, while according to definition 2, perm-q refers to d=qn, which leads to n=d=1 and it’s a naïve configuration. By clarifying the assumptions made in this section, the reader can better understand the example and its implications for IST.

Limitations:
Future works are discussed in the conclusion section.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.summary: This submission presents a theoretical analysis for the independent subnetwork training (IST) algorithm for training models under both data and model parallel settings. The convergence guarantees are analyzed for quadratic loss functions, when using permutation sketches as the model compressors.
reviews.strengths:
- Optimization using data and model parallelization is an important practical problem, for which more theoretical insights are welcome 
- The submission presents a fairly thorough analysis of the convergence guarantees for IST for the quadratic loss function analyzed
- The authors highlight some limitations of IST, which would be useful to be aware of (e.g. the fact that in the general case of the quadratic function the algorithm does not converge to the solution)
- The submission is overall well written, and the authors are careful to introduce the setup and all the assumptions used in their analysis
reviews.weaknesses:
- Overall the analysis presented in this submission is very limited, as it only deals with a specific type of loss function, which is not a practical instance where model parallelization would be useful
- While the authors argue that the quadratic model has been previously used in the literature for studying neural networks (lines 140-144), in my understanding this model still relies on a Taylor approximation for the loss function of a neural network (for non-linear models). It is therefore not clear how the error introduced through this approximation would translate into the convergence analysis presented in the submission
- The authors use additional simplifying assumptions, such as the fact that each node can compute the true gradient of its submodel, which would be infeasible in the case of large datasets. Additionally, the results are only presented for Perm-1 sketches when the number of nodes matches the dimension of the model, which is again not a practical use-case. While the authors argue that their results can generalize beyond these limitations, a more general formulation is not provided in the submission.
- Other works have analyzed the convergence guarantees for IST, notably [28] has done so for a one hidden layer network with ReLU activations, which is a more general case than the one from this submission. It is not clear what are the additional insights presented here, compared to the previous work.
reviews.questions:
- Can the authors please detail how their choice of the quadratic loss function ties to practical applications of model parallelism (e.g. for neural networks)?
- In Equation (12), do the matrices $L_i$ also need to be semi-positive definite?
- For Theorems 1 and 2, can the authors please describe how the results would change when using an unbiased gradient estimator, instead of the full gradient for each node?
- Are there any situations, without preconditioning, which would satisfy the conditions from Theorem 1, for model parallelization? The Identity example from Section 3.1 seems to only apply to data parallelization. 
- For Equation (23), I am confused by the notation for the scaling coefficient. Should it be $[(L_i)_{\pi_i, \pi_i}]^{-1/2 }$ instead?
- From the analysis in Section 3.1.2 it looks like the fact that $C_i$ is biased does not have any effect on the bound. Can the authors provide more insights on why that is the case?
- In Appendix B.4. the authors show generalizations for $d>n$ and for different sketches. I think these would be useful to highlight in the main submission, since they correspond to a more practical setting.
reviews.limitations:
I believe the authors have properly addressed the limitations of their analysis. However, I am not convinced that the contributions presented in this submission are broad enough, which ultimately motivates my score.


---------------------------------
**Edited after rebuttal**

After reading the authors' answers, and the other reviews, I decided to increase my overall rating to 5, as well as the scores for Soundness, Presentation and Contribution.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This submission presents a theoretical analysis for the independent subnetwork training (IST) algorithm for training models under both data and model parallel settings. The convergence guarantees are analyzed for quadratic loss functions, when using permutation sketches as the model compressors.

Strengths:
- Optimization using data and model parallelization is an important practical problem, for which more theoretical insights are welcome 
- The submission presents a fairly thorough analysis of the convergence guarantees for IST for the quadratic loss function analyzed
- The authors highlight some limitations of IST, which would be useful to be aware of (e.g. the fact that in the general case of the quadratic function the algorithm does not converge to the solution)
- The submission is overall well written, and the authors are careful to introduce the setup and all the assumptions used in their analysis

Weaknesses:
- Overall the analysis presented in this submission is very limited, as it only deals with a specific type of loss function, which is not a practical instance where model parallelization would be useful
- While the authors argue that the quadratic model has been previously used in the literature for studying neural networks (lines 140-144), in my understanding this model still relies on a Taylor approximation for the loss function of a neural network (for non-linear models). It is therefore not clear how the error introduced through this approximation would translate into the convergence analysis presented in the submission
- The authors use additional simplifying assumptions, such as the fact that each node can compute the true gradient of its submodel, which would be infeasible in the case of large datasets. Additionally, the results are only presented for Perm-1 sketches when the number of nodes matches the dimension of the model, which is again not a practical use-case. While the authors argue that their results can generalize beyond these limitations, a more general formulation is not provided in the submission.
- Other works have analyzed the convergence guarantees for IST, notably [28] has done so for a one hidden layer network with ReLU activations, which is a more general case than the one from this submission. It is not clear what are the additional insights presented here, compared to the previous work.

Questions:
- Can the authors please detail how their choice of the quadratic loss function ties to practical applications of model parallelism (e.g. for neural networks)?
- In Equation (12), do the matrices $L_i$ also need to be semi-positive definite?
- For Theorems 1 and 2, can the authors please describe how the results would change when using an unbiased gradient estimator, instead of the full gradient for each node?
- Are there any situations, without preconditioning, which would satisfy the conditions from Theorem 1, for model parallelization? The Identity example from Section 3.1 seems to only apply to data parallelization. 
- For Equation (23), I am confused by the notation for the scaling coefficient. Should it be $[(L_i)_{\pi_i, \pi_i}]^{-1/2 }$ instead?
- From the analysis in Section 3.1.2 it looks like the fact that $C_i$ is biased does not have any effect on the bound. Can the authors provide more insights on why that is the case?
- In Appendix B.4. the authors show generalizations for $d>n$ and for different sketches. I think these would be useful to highlight in the main submission, since they correspond to a more practical setting.

Limitations:
I believe the authors have properly addressed the limitations of their analysis. However, I am not convinced that the contributions presented in this submission are broad enough, which ultimately motivates my score.


---------------------------------
**Edited after rebuttal**

After reading the authors' answers, and the other reviews, I decided to increase my overall rating to 5, as well as the scores for Soundness, Presentation and Contribution.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
### Expanded Review  

**Summary:**  
This submission presents a rigorous theoretical analysis of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST), focusing on its optimization properties for quadratic loss functions under data and model parallelism. The authors leverage permutation sketches to formalize IST as a compressed gradient method (Algorithm 1, Eq. 6) and derive convergence guarantees for homogeneous and heterogeneous settings. Key contributions include characterizing irreducible bias in the presence of linear terms (Sec. 4) and identifying preconditioning strategies to enforce convergence conditions (Sec. 3.1). While the analysis offers novel insights into IST's optimization dynamics, its reliance on simplified problem settings limits practical applicability.  

---

### Strengths:  
1. **Problem Relevance**: The theoretical scrutiny of IST addresses a critical gap in distributed optimization literature, especially given IST's empirical success in scaling large models (e.g., ResNets [14], GCNs [43]). The quadratics-based framework (Sec. 2, Eq. 12) provides a tractable testbed for isolating IST-specific challenges, such as gradient estimator misalignment (Sec. 3.1, Remark 1).  
2. **Technical Rigor**: The convergence analysis is thorough, employing matrix smoothness (Assumption 1) and weighted norms to capture subspace-dependent dynamics (Theorem 1, Eq. 15–16). The distinction between homogeneous and heterogeneous settings (Secs. 3.1.1–3.1.2) clarifies how diagonal preconditioning mitigates issues like indefinite matrices (Eq. 17–21).  
3. **Bias Characterization**: Section 4 compellingly demonstrates IST's non-vanishing bias (Eq. 27–28) and its dependence on data heterogeneity (*h* ∝ *b* − *D̃b*), contrasting sharply with unbiased methods (e.g., compressed SGD [3, 27]). The non-asymptotic bound in Theorem 2 (Eq. 30) quantifies the trade-off between convergence speed and neighborhood size.  
4. **Clarity**: The manuscript is well structured, with clear formalism (Sec. 2) and accessible examples (e.g., identity/permuation sketches in Sec. 3.1).  

---

### Weaknesses:  
1. **Limited Scope of Loss Functions**: The restriction to quadratic models (Eq. 12) significantly narrows the applicability of results. While quadratics offer analytical tractability (lines 140–144), they poorly approximate neural network loss landscapes, which exhibit non-convexity, saddle points, and complex curvature. **For instance, the Taylor approximation argument (lines 141–142) ignores higher-order terms critical to neural network optimization, making it unclear how Theorem 2’s neighborhood bias translates to practical IST deployments.**  
2. **Overly Simplistic Assumptions**:  
   - **Gradient Computation**: Assuming nodes compute exact submodel gradients (Sec. 3, point 1) overlooks stochasticity and data sharding, which are fundamental to distributed training. **The analysis would benefit from incorporating stochastic gradients, as hinted in Sec. 3 (point 1), but no such extension is provided.**  
   - **Sketch Constraints**: Perm-1 sketches require *d* = *n* (Def. 2), which is impractical for large-scale models (e.g., *d* ≫ *n* in transformers). Though Appendix B.4 generalizes to *d* > *n*, **these critical extensions are relegated to supplements, diminishing the main text’s utility.**  
3. **Marginal Advancements Over Prior Work**: Compared to [28]’s analysis of single-hidden-layer ReLU networks, this work’s quadratic focus (Sec. 3) offers narrower insights. **While the bias analysis (Sec. 4) is novel, it does not clearly surpass [28] in explaining IST’s efficacy for neural networks.**  

---

### Questions:  
1. **Quadratic Models vs. Neural Networks**: How do the theoretical phenomena observed (e.g., irreducible bias in Eq. 30) manifest in neural network training? **Can the authors provide empirical evidence (beyond Appendix experiments) linking quadratic theory to neural network behaviors?**  
2. **Positive Definiteness**: Must *Lᵢ* in Eq. 12 be positive definite? **If not, how does indefiniteness impact Theorem 1’s convergence conditions (Eq. 14)?**  
3. **Stochastic Gradients**: How would Theorem 1 change if unbiased gradient estimators replaced exact gradients? **Would the *O(1/K)* rate (Eq. 15) degrade under gradient noise?**  
4. **Practical Feasibility of Preconditioning**: For heterogeneous preconditioning (Eq. 23), scaling sketches by *[Lᵢ]ᴨᵢ,ᴨᵢ*⁻¹/² requires global knowledge of local diagonals *Lᵢ*. **How can this be implemented in privacy-sensitive settings (e.g., federated learning) without violating data locality?**  
5. **Biased Compressors**: Section 3.1.2 uses biased compressors (*C̃ᵢ*), yet Theorem 2’s neighborhood term depends only on *h* and *σ*² (not bias). **Why does compressor bias not appear in Eq. 30, and does this imply bias is benign?**  
6. **Generalization of Results**: The submission notes generalizability beyond *d* = *n* (Appendix B.4). **Why are results for *d* > *n* or overlapping sketches (e.g., Rand-*q*) not included in the main text?**  

---

### Limitations:  
The authors rightly acknowledge simplifications (single-step gradients, quadratics), but deeper limitations persist:  
- **Federated Learning Gaps**: While IST is applied to federated settings via asynchronous dropout [13], the analysis assumes homogeneous compute (Sec. 2), omitting client heterogeneity and partial participation.  
- **Communication-Computation Trade-offs**: Perm-1 reduces communication costs via sparse updates (Sec. 3.1.2), but **the analysis ignores aggregation overheads (Eq. 6) or straggler effects**, which are critical in practice.  
- **Comparison to Alternatives**: Claims of superiority over communication compression (Sec. 4.3) are inadequately substantiated. **For example, how does IST’s bias (Eq. 30) compare to the variance-induced neighborhoods of QSGD [3]?**  

---

### Edited After Rebuttal:  
The authors’ response clarified extensions to *d* > *n* and stochastic gradients, addressed positivedefiniteness concerns, and emphasized the quadratics model’s role in isolating optimization phenomena. This, coupled with the bias analysis’s novelty, justifies a **borderline accept** rating.  

---

**Rating:**  
**5: Borderline accept**  
The paper provides a technically sound analysis of IST for quadratic objectives, identifying convergence conditions and inherent biases. While the simplified setting limits immediate applicability, the core theoretical contributions (e.g., preconditioning strategies, bias characterization) offer valuable foundations for future work. Strengths in rigor and clarity marginally outweigh weaknesses in scope.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This submission presents a theoretical analysis for the independent subnetwork training (IST) algorithm for training models under both data and model parallel settings. The convergence guarantees are analyzed for quadratic loss functions, when using permutation sketches as the model compressors.

Strengths:
- Optimization using data and model parallelization is an important practical problem, for which more theoretical insights are welcome 
- The submission presents a fairly thorough analysis of the convergence guarantees for IST for the quadratic loss function analyzed
- The authors highlight some limitations of IST, which would be useful to be aware of (e.g. the fact that in the general case of the quadratic function the algorithm does not converge to the solution)
- The submission is overall well written, and the authors are careful to introduce the setup and all the assumptions used in their analysis

Weaknesses:
- Overall the analysis presented in this submission is very limited, as it only deals with a specific type of loss function, which is not a practical instance where model parallelization would be useful
- While the authors argue that the quadratic model has been previously used in the literature for studying neural networks (lines 140-144), in my understanding this model still relies on a Taylor approximation for the loss function of a neural network (for non-linear models). It is therefore not clear how the error introduced through this approximation would translate into the convergence analysis presented in the submission
- The authors use additional simplifying assumptions, such as the fact that each node can compute the true gradient of its submodel, which would be infeasible in the case of large datasets. Additionally, the results are only presented for Perm-1 sketches when the number of nodes matches the dimension of the model, which is again not a practical use-case. While the authors argue that their results can generalize beyond these limitations, a more general formulation is not provided in the submission.
- Other works have analyzed the convergence guarantees for IST, notably [28] has done so for a one hidden layer network with ReLU activations, which is a more general case than the one from this submission. It is not clear what are the additional insights presented here, compared to the previous work.

Questions:
- Can the authors please detail how their choice of the quadratic loss function ties to practical applications of model parallelism (e.g. for neural networks)?
- In Equation (12), do the matrices $L_i$ also need to be semi-positive definite?
- For Theorems 1 and 2, can the authors please describe how the results would change when using an unbiased gradient estimator, instead of the full gradient for each node?
- Are there any situations, without preconditioning, which would satisfy the conditions from Theorem 1, for model parallelization? The Identity example from Section 3.1 seems to only apply to data parallelization. 
- For Equation (23), I am confused by the notation for the scaling coefficient. Should it be $[(L_i)_{\pi_i, \pi_i}]^{-1/2 }$ instead?
- From the analysis in Section 3.1.2 it looks like the fact that $C_i$ is biased does not have any effect on the bound. Can the authors provide more insights on why that is the case?
- In Appendix B.4. the authors show generalizations for $d>n$ and for different sketches. I think these would be useful to highlight in the main submission, since they correspond to a more practical setting.

Limitations:
I believe the authors have properly addressed the limitations of their analysis. However, I am not convinced that the contributions presented in this submission are broad enough, which ultimately motivates my score.


---------------------------------
**Edited after rebuttal**

After reading the authors' answers, and the other reviews, I decided to increase my overall rating to 5, as well as the scores for Soundness, Presentation and Contribution.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.original_rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: Attribute Based Interpretable Evaluation Metrics for Generative Models

reviews.summary: Paper proposes an evaluation metric for generative models which compare the distributions of real and generated images using a predefined set of attributes, or pairwise occurrences of attributes. The advantage of these metrics over the previous work is that they provide explicit visibility of which aspects contribute to the final metric value.
reviews.strengths: The idea for the proposed metric is novel – it uses language-image models to measure alignment of distributions of real and generated images given a set of text attributes. Additionally, the metric is highly customizable for downstream tasks because users may define their own set of attributes that are of interest to the specific task and drop irrelevant attributes.
reviews.weaknesses:
Failure modes of the metric are not discussed (or limitations of language-image models and how they affect the metric). 

Consider a scenario where there are two models, A and B, with the same SaKLD. Model A produces essentially perfect alignment on all attributes other than one which fails dramatically, causing a large spike in SaKLD histogram, and this attribute is the only contributing to the final score. Model B on the other hand performs poorly across all attributes but averaging over attributes yields the same SaKLD score as the model A. In this scenario SaKLD would potentially not agree with human judgment, since failing in a single attribute might not be visible when inspecting large image grids. Can this kind of scenario occur in practice, and if it can, what would be your recommendation for the user of the metric in that case?

Fig. 4 shows that SaKLD and PaKLD are dominated by few attributes of attribute pairs. Is this usually the case in practice? Fig. 5 (b) also indicates that adding new attributes contribute to the metric with diminishing strength. This might be misleading for the user of the metric. Intuitively, adding a large set of attributes should correspond to more thorough evaluation of the model, however, this might not be the case if few attributes are dominating the final value of the metric.

The empirical effectiveness of the attribute based metric is not fully demonstrated. The authors advocated for an interpretable metric but unfortunately end up comparing modern generative models using single scalar numbers (as the existing metrics do), instead of taking advantage of the interpretability of the metric and showing a more fine-grained analysis of the models.
reviews.questions:
1. Fig. 2 shows CS and DCS values for distinct attributes, how would these scores look like for pairs of “opposite attributes”, e.g., “long hair - short hair” or “makeup - no makeup”? Would DCS values be symmetric around zero or something else?

2. The description of Tab. 1 was slightly unclear, how is Tab. 1 exactly calculated? The numerical values seem to be relatively close to each other, is the difference here significant? Tab. 1 shows mean accuracy but what does the accuracy distribution looks like?

3. Automatic extractions of text attributes is an interesting idea, how robust BLIP is with various datasets? What are the most frequently extracted attributes?

Minor notes:

4. Sec. 3.3 a capital letter is missing
5. Line 154: Needs to be “the center of the images”
6. Line 220: $n$ and $P_2$ are not defined
7. Tab. 3 would benefit from also having a column for FID to see how SaKLD and PaKLD differ from it
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations of their work.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
Paper proposes an evaluation metric for generative models which compare the distributions of real and generated images using a predefined set of attributes, or pairwise occurrences of attributes. The advantage of these metrics over the previous work is that they provide explicit visibility of which aspects contribute to the final metric value.

Strengths:
The idea for the proposed metric is novel – it uses language-image models to measure alignment of distributions of real and generated images given a set of text attributes. Additionally, the metric is highly customizable for downstream tasks because users may define their own set of attributes that are of interest to the specific task and drop irrelevant attributes.

Weaknesses:
Failure modes of the metric are not discussed (or limitations of language-image models and how they affect the metric). 

Consider a scenario where there are two models, A and B, with the same SaKLD. Model A produces essentially perfect alignment on all attributes other than one which fails dramatically, causing a large spike in SaKLD histogram, and this attribute is the only contributing to the final score. Model B on the other hand performs poorly across all attributes but averaging over attributes yields the same SaKLD score as the model A. In this scenario SaKLD would potentially not agree with human judgment, since failing in a single attribute might not be visible when inspecting large image grids. Can this kind of scenario occur in practice, and if it can, what would be your recommendation for the user of the metric in that case?

Fig. 4 shows that SaKLD and PaKLD are dominated by few attributes of attribute pairs. Is this usually the case in practice? Fig. 5 (b) also indicates that adding new attributes contribute to the metric with diminishing strength. This might be misleading for the user of the metric. Intuitively, adding a large set of attributes should correspond to more thorough evaluation of the model, however, this might not be the case if few attributes are dominating the final value of the metric.

The empirical effectiveness of the attribute based metric is not fully demonstrated. The authors advocated for an interpretable metric but unfortunately end up comparing modern generative models using single scalar numbers (as the existing metrics do), instead of taking advantage of the interpretability of the metric and showing a more fine-grained analysis of the models.

Questions:
1. Fig. 2 shows CS and DCS values for distinct attributes, how would these scores look like for pairs of “opposite attributes”, e.g., “long hair - short hair” or “makeup - no makeup”? Would DCS values be symmetric around zero or something else?

2. The description of Tab. 1 was slightly unclear, how is Tab. 1 exactly calculated? The numerical values seem to be relatively close to each other, is the difference here significant? Tab. 1 shows mean accuracy but what does the accuracy distribution looks like?

3. Automatic extractions of text attributes is an interesting idea, how robust BLIP is with various datasets? What are the most frequently extracted attributes?

Minor notes:

4. Sec. 3.3 a capital letter is missing
5. Line 154: Needs to be “the center of the images”
6. Line 220: $n$ and $P_2$ are not defined
7. Tab. 3 would benefit from also having a column for FID to see how SaKLD and PaKLD differ from it

Limitations:
The authors adequately addressed the limitations of their work.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
### Expanded Peer Review

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a novel evaluation framework for generative models that leverages attribute-driven embeddings derived from language-image models (CLIP) to assess distributional alignment between real and generated images. The core innovation lies in two new metrics: Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD), which quantifies distributional differences for individual attributes (e.g., "eyeglasses," "smiling"), and Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD), which evaluates correlations between attribute pairs (e.g., "woman" and "smiling"). The authors position these metrics as interpretable alternatives to established measures like FID, arguing that they provide granular insights into *why* a model underperforms (e.g., bias in "long hair" or missing "hats"). Experiments validate sensitivity to attribute shifts (Sec. 5.1-5.2) and reveal dataset-dependent performance gaps between GANs and diffusion models (Sec. 5.3). While the technical approach is sound, the paper could better leverage the promised interpretability in its analysis.

**Strengths:**  
1. **Novelty in Attribute-Driven Evaluation:** The core idea—using CLIP-derived attribute embeddings to decompose distributional similarity—is a significant departure from opaque embedding spaces like Inception-V3. The proposed Directional CLIPScore (DCS) (Sec. 3.2) effectively addresses CLIPScore’s limited discriminability (Fig. 2, Table 1) by centering similarity calculations on dataset-specific means (CX, CT). This enables interpretable per-attribute scoring (e.g., DCS values intuitively indicate "makeup" intensity in Fig. 2b).  
2. **Customizability for Downstream Tasks:** The flexibility in attribute selection (BLIP extraction, user annotation, GPT generation; Sec. 3.3) is a major strength. Users can tailor metrics to domain-specific attributes (e.g., facial features in CelebA or object properties in LSUN Cat), making the framework adaptable beyond the tested datasets. This addresses the paper’s critique of one-size-fits-all metrics like FID.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Insufficient Discussion of Failure Modes:** The paper overlooks critical limitations of language-image models that could propagate into the metrics. For instance:  
   - DCS relies on CLIP’s attribute recognition, which may fail for abstract or culturally nuanced concepts (e.g., "artistic style" in Fig. 1). If CLIP misjudges "makeup" intensity in generated images, SaKLD/PaKLD would inherit this error.  
   - The scenario where SaKLD masks critical failures (raised in the original review) is plausible. Suppose Model A perfectly matches 19/20 attributes but catastrophically fails on "eyeglasses" (e.g., generates none), while Model B has moderate errors across all attributes. SaKLD could average to the same value, misleading users. The authors should recommend inspecting per-attribute histograms (e.g., Fig. 4a) to mitigate this.  
2. **Dominance by Few Attributes Diminishes Interpretability:** As noted, Fig. 4 shows SaKLD/PaKLD are often dominated by a subset of attributes/pairs (e.g., "smiling" in SaKLD, "woman ∩ smiling" in PaKLD). This risks "interpretability overload"—users may struggle to extract actionable insights if 80% of the score stems from 20% of attributes. Fig. 5b exacerbates this: adding attributes beyond 20 has minimal impact on model rankings, suggesting diminishing returns. The framework would benefit from attribute importance weighting (e.g., entropy-based).  
3. **Underutilized Interpretability in Analysis:** While the metrics are touted as explainable, key results revert to scalar summaries. For example:  
   - Table 3 reports SaKLD/PaKLD scores for SOTA models but does *not* use per-attribute breakdowns to explain *why* iDDPM lags on LSUN Cat (e.g., is it poor color diversity? shape fidelity?). Table 4’s color/shape analysis is a step forward but is buried in a subsection.  
   - The finding that diffusion models struggle with color attributes (Sec. 5.3) aligns with Khrulkov’s optimal transport theory but lacks visual examples (e.g., generated images with mismatched "yellow fur").  

**Questions:**  
1. **DCS for Antonymic Attributes:** For opposites like "long hair"/"short hair," DCS values should be symmetric around zero if the dataset is balanced. In Fig. 2b, DCS("mustache") = -7.6 suggests negative scores may indicate antonymic relationships (e.g., "no mustache"). The paper should clarify this and show distributions for such pairs.  
2. **Table 1 Calculation Details:** The description "accuracy of positive samples with the highest score" is ambiguous. Based on Sec. 3.2, it appears top-k DCS values for an attribute (e.g., "smiling") were compared to CelebA ground-truth labels. The small difference (0.395 CS vs. 0.409 DCS) needs statistical validation (e.g., p-values), as the improvement may not be significant for all attributes.  
3. **BLIP Robustness Across Datasets:** BLIP’s attribute extraction (Sec. 3.3) is only tested on human faces (CelebA, FFHQ). How does it handle diverse domains like LSUN Cat? Are extracted attributes (e.g., "pointy ears") meaningful, or does it generate noise (e.g., "blurry background")? Listing top BLIP attributes for each dataset would clarify this.  
4. **FID Comparison in Table 3:** Adding FID scores alongside SaKLD/PaKLD is essential to contextualize claims. For instance, if StyleGAN2-ADA has low FID but high SaKLD (Table 3 FFHQ), this would starkly illustrate how data augmentation harms attribute diversity—a compelling case for the new metrics.  

**Minor Notes:**  
- Sec. 3.3: Capitalize "Attribute Selection Methodologies."  
- Line 154: Correct to "the center of the images."  
- Line 220: Define \(M = \binom{n}{2}\) (combinations of attribute pairs).  
- Table 3: Include sample sizes for MetFaces (50k generated vs. 1,336 real images) to clarify comparability.  

**Limitations:**  
The authors appropriately note limitations (e.g., ambiguity in complex images). However, the reliance on CLIP/BLIP introduces dataset bias—these models inherit priors from their web-scale training data, which may misrepresent niche domains (e.g., medical images). Testing on non-photographic data (e.g., sketches) would strengthen claims about generalizability.  

**Decision:**  
**6: Weak Accept**  
The paper makes a technically sound contribution by reframing generative evaluation around interpretable attributes. The DCS embedding and SaKLD/PaKLD metrics are novel solutions to the "black-box" critique of existing metrics. However, the analysis undersells the promised interpretability, and failure modes of language-model dependencies need deeper discussion. With minor revisions (e.g., per-attribute visualizations, FID comparisons), this could be a strong contribution. The core methodology is robust and merits acceptance.

Summary:
Paper proposes an evaluation metric for generative models which compare the distributions of real and generated images using a predefined set of attributes, or pairwise occurrences of attributes. The advantage of these metrics over the previous work is that they provide explicit visibility of which aspects contribute to the final metric value.

Strengths:
The idea for the proposed metric is novel – it uses language-image models to measure alignment of distributions of real and generated images given a set of text attributes. Additionally, the metric is highly customizable for downstream tasks because users may define their own set of attributes that are of interest to the specific task and drop irrelevant attributes.

Consider a scenario where there are two models, A and B, with the same SaKLD. Model B on the other hand performs poorly across all attributes but averaging over attributes yields the same SaKLD score as the model A. In this scenario SaKLD would potentially not agree with human judgment, since failing in a single attribute might not be visible when inspecting large image grids. Can this kind of scenario occur in practice, and if it can, what would be your recommendation for the user of the metric in that case?

Fig. 4 shows that SaKLD and PaKLD are dominated by few attributes of attribute pairs. Is this usually the case in practice? Fig. 5 (b) also indicates that adding new attributes contribute to the metric with diminishing strength. This might be misleading for the user of the metric. Minor notes:
