reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes two new metrics allowing to measure and explain the diversity of a generated set of images w.r.t a training set. Instead of the usual distributional distances relying upon an embedding space from a pre-trained model, these metrics rely on a set of textual attributes. The similarity between an image and an attribute is computed using their representation in a common semantic space, via the CLIP model - vectors are shifted using a centre of training images/attributes to make similarity scores more meaningful. Several ways to obtain attributes (Captioning, User-based or GPT-based) are investigated. The usefulness of the metrics is tested with an experiment injecting images correlated with target attributes in data, an experiment aiming to detect a specific attribute relationship in a curated dataset, and in a comparison of several generative models.
reviews.strengths:
- This paper proposes an interesting approach, aiming at using the common semantic space between images and text proposed by CLIP to measure attribute and image relatedness, in order to provide interpretable representations of generated images and measure attribute-based metrics between a reference and generated dataset; such an application seems relatively original to me. 
- The two first experiments demonstrate well the usefulness of the approach, able to detect a shift when the data has been curated by human, based on which attributes.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The presentation of the paper could be improved upon. This include the writing, and the readability of the figures, as well as the quantity of information provided. This last points concerns mainly the presentation and framing of the problem of interpretability of representations, as well as the presentation and motivation of the experimental settings. 
- In particular, the paper lacks related work on concept-based representation for interpretability of images. While building metrics dedicated to generative model seems new to me, there are based on an idea which has been explored extensively before. See for example "Concept Whitening for Interpretable Image Recognition, Chen et al, 2020". 
- The paper focuses on a narrow choice of methods to generate attributes, which, to me, should be one of the key experimental investigation of the paper. Notably, the previous literature explores using different kind of attributes, coming from existing data (for example, "Interpretable Basis Decomposition for Visual Explanation, Zhou et al, 2018") or to be learnt ("A Framework to Learn with Interpretation", Parekh et al, 2021). The authors only (very shortly) argue about the number of needed attributes.
- The toy experiments seem relevant but are very fastly presented and should be expanded upon. The remaining experiments are too short to be convincing and only focus on a handful of models.
reviews.questions:
- Could you better formalize the notation $DCS_{\mathcal{X}}(a_{i,j})$ for joint distributions ?
- Is it necessary to center the text and images differently ? They lie in the same semantic space. Also, would it not be reasonable to be using the attributes themselves, rather than captions ?
reviews.limitations: - Previous distributional metrics are several times referred as relying on external models in your paper. However, the attributes that you use also rely on external models (except the USER one, of course - but in this case, the computing of DCS still relies on a captioning model). How would you address this issue ?
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes two new metrics allowing to measure and explain the diversity of a generated set of images w.r.t a training set. Instead of the usual distributional distances relying upon an embedding space from a pre-trained model, these metrics rely on a set of textual attributes. The similarity between an image and an attribute is computed using their representation in a common semantic space, via the CLIP model - vectors are shifted using a centre of training images/attributes to make similarity scores more meaningful. Several ways to obtain attributes (Captioning, User-based or GPT-based) are investigated. The usefulness of the metrics is tested with an experiment injecting images correlated with target attributes in data, an experiment aiming to detect a specific attribute relationship in a curated dataset, and in a comparison of several generative models.

Strengths:
- This paper proposes an interesting approach, aiming at using the common semantic space between images and text proposed by CLIP to measure attribute and image relatedness, in order to provide interpretable representations of generated images and measure attribute-based metrics between a reference and generated dataset; such an application seems relatively original to me. 
- The two first experiments demonstrate well the usefulness of the approach, able to detect a shift when the data has been curated by human, based on which attributes.

Weaknesses:
- The presentation of the paper could be improved upon. This include the writing, and the readability of the figures, as well as the quantity of information provided. This last points concerns mainly the presentation and framing of the problem of interpretability of representations, as well as the presentation and motivation of the experimental settings. 
- In particular, the paper lacks related work on concept-based representation for interpretability of images. While building metrics dedicated to generative model seems new to me, there are based on an idea which has been explored extensively before. See for example "Concept Whitening for Interpretable Image Recognition, Chen et al, 2020". 
- The paper focuses on a narrow choice of methods to generate attributes, which, to me, should be one of the key experimental investigation of the paper. Notably, the previous literature explores using different kind of attributes, coming from existing data (for example, "Interpretable Basis Decomposition for Visual Explanation, Zhou et al, 2018") or to be learnt ("A Framework to Learn with Interpretation", Parekh et al, 2021). The authors only (very shortly) argue about the number of needed attributes.
- The toy experiments seem relevant but are very fastly presented and should be expanded upon. The remaining experiments are too short to be convincing and only focus on a handful of models.

Questions:
- Could you better formalize the notation $DCS_{\mathcal{X}}(a_{i,j})$ for joint distributions ?
- Is it necessary to center the text and images differently ? They lie in the same semantic space. Also, would it not be reasonable to be using the attributes themselves, rather than captions ?

Limitations:
- Previous distributional metrics are several times referred as relying on external models in your paper. However, the attributes that you use also rely on external models (except the USER one, of course - but in this case, the computing of DCS still relies on a captioning model). How would you address this issue ?

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

### Summary  
This paper introduces two novel metrics, SaKLD and PaKLD, designed to measure and explain the diversity of generated image sets relative to a training dataset. Departing from conventional distributional distances in pre-trained embedding spaces, these metrics leverage textual attributes quantified via CLIP-based representations. The authors propose Directional CLIPScore (DCS)—which centers image and attribute embeddings using dataset-specific means (Equations 2–4)—to enhance interpretability. Attributes are sourced via captioning (BLIP), human annotation (CelebA), or GPT-3 queries. Experiments include: (1) correlated-image injection to validate metric sensitivity (Table 2), (2) a toy experiment demonstrating PaKLD’s advantage in detecting attribute relationships (Figure 4), and (3) a comparison of GANs and diffusion models across datasets (Tables 3–4).  

### Strengths  
1. **Innovative Approach**: The paper’s core contribution—using CLIP’s semantic space to quantify attribute-image alignment—addresses a critical gap in interpretable evaluation for generative models. The shift from opaque embeddings (e.g., Inception-V3 in FID) to attribute-driven metrics (SaKLD/PaKLD) is a meaningful advance. The DCS formulation (centering embeddings via \(C_X\) and \(C_T\)) effectively mitigates CLIPScore’s narrow value distribution (Table 1, Figure 3), enabling more discriminative attribute quantification.  
2. **Empirical Validation**: The correlated-image injection experiment (Table 2) robustly demonstrates SaKLD/PaKLD’s sensitivity to attribute shifts, outperforming FID in detecting biases (e.g., injected "man with makeup" images). The toy experiment (Figure 4) elegantly showcases PaKLD’s unique ability to expose broken attribute correlations (e.g., "smiling men" vs. "non-smiling women"), which SaKLD misses.  
3. **Practical Insights**: The model comparison (Tables 3–4) reveals compelling findings—e.g., StyleGAN2-ADA’s poor attribute fidelity despite strong FID scores, and diffusion models’ (iDDPM) weakness in color attributes due to optimal transport limitations (Section 5.3). These insights underscore the value of attribute-centric evaluation beyond traditional metrics.  

### Weaknesses  
1. **Presentation and Clarity**:  
   - The manuscript suffers from ambiguous phrasing in key sections. For example, the abstract’s claim that existing metrics "measure distributional distance [...] without considering interpretability" oversimplifies; while true of FID, newer metrics (e.g., precision/recall) partially address this but are not engaged with.  
   - Figures are inadequately explained: Figure 1’s "Model 2" bias (only women) is unclear without visual examples, and Figure 5’s ablation curves lack context (e.g., why does iDDPM degrade with more attributes?).  
   - Terminology like "interpretable embedding" (Section 3) is used loosely. While DCS values correlate with attributes (Table 1), their human intuitiveness (e.g., is DCS=8.2 for "makeup" meaningful?) is undemonstrated.  

2. **Underdeveloped Related Work**:  
   - The paper neglects extensive literature on concept-based interpretability. For instance, *concept activation vectors* (TCAV, Kim et al. 2018) and *concept whitening* (Chen et al. 2020) directly learn human-understandable concepts in embeddings but are never discussed. This gap weakens the positioning of DCS as a novel "interpretable embedding."  
   - Attribute generation methods are narrowly scoped. Beyond BLIP/GPT-3/user labels, prior work (e.g., Zhou et al.’s basis decomposition for visual concepts) offers alternative approaches. The manuscript dismisses attribute count ablation (Figure 5b) with minimal analysis, despite its critical impact on metric behavior.  

3. **Limited Experimental Depth**:  
   - The generative model comparison (Table 3) evaluates only six models across three datasets. Key claims—e.g., diffusion models’ inferiority on color attributes (Table 4)—rely on a single dataset (LSUN Cat) and one model (iDDPM). Broader validation (e.g., testing Stable Diffusion) is absent.  
   - The "curated subsets" experiment (Section 5.2) uses artificial data (20k "smiling men"). Real-world validation on standard benchmarks (e.g., biased generators in StyleGAN) is missing.  
   - Computational costs are unaddressed: DCS requires BLIP captioning and CLIP inference for all images, making it orders of magnitude slower than FID.  

### Questions  
1. **Formalization of \(DCS_{\mathcal{X}}(a_{i,j})\)**: The joint distribution for paired attributes (Equation 6) is ambiguously defined. Is \(DCS_{\mathcal{X}}(a_{i,j})\) the joint PDF of *two* DCS values (e.g., DCS for "smiling" and DCS for "male")? Clarification is essential for reproducibility.  
2. **Centering Strategy**: Why are separate centers \(C_X\) (image mean) and \(C_T\) (caption mean) used (Equation 2)? Since CLIP aligns images/text in one space, a unified center (e.g., all training embeddings) might be more consistent. Have alternatives been explored?  
3. **Attribute Selection Impact**: How do different attribute sets (BLIP vs. GPT-3) affect metric stability? Figure 5b suggests iDDPM degrades with more attributes—is this consistent across selection methods?  

### Limitations  
1. **Dependency on External Models**: While critiquing metrics like FID for relying on pre-trained networks, SaKLD/PaKLD depend entirely on CLIP, BLIP, and GPT-3. This inherits biases—e.g., CLIP’s skewed gender representations (Radford et al. 2021). The "USER" setting (CelebA attributes) mitigates this but still uses CLIP for DCS. The paper does not address this contradiction.  
2. **Domain Specificity**: The approach falters for datasets without clear attributes (e.g., landscapes, abstract art), as acknowledged in Section 6. However, no fallback strategy or hybrid metric is proposed.  
3. **Scalability**: Evaluating 50k images (Figure 5a) is computationally intensive. Have the authors considered approximations (e.g., subsampling) for large-scale use?  

### Rating  
**6: Weak Accept**  
This work presents a technically solid contribution: interpretable metrics (SaKLD/PaKLD) that successfully expose attribute-level biases in generative models, validated through controlled experiments. The DCS formulation and attribute-pair analysis are novel and impactful. However, the paper’s impact is tempered by presentation issues, incomplete related work, and narrow experimental validation. Addressing these—particularly by expanding comparisons and engaging with concept-based interpretability literature—would strengthen it significantly. For now, it represents a moderate but valuable step toward explainable evaluation.  

---  
**Decision**: Weak Accept (6)
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes two new metrics allowing to measure and explain the diversity of a generated set of images w.r.t a training set. Instead of the usual distributional distances relying upon an embedding space from a pre-trained model, these metrics rely on a set of textual attributes. The similarity between an image and an attribute is computed using their representation in a common semantic space, via the CLIP model - vectors are shifted using a centre of training images/attributes to make similarity scores more meaningful. Several ways to obtain attributes (Captioning, User-based or GPT-based) are investigated. The usefulness of the metrics is tested with an experiment injecting images correlated with target attributes in data, an experiment aiming to detect a specific attribute relationship in a curated dataset, and in a comparison of several generative models.

Strengths:
- This paper proposes an interesting approach, aiming at using the common semantic space between images and text proposed by CLIP to measure attribute and image relatedness, in order to provide interpretable representations of generated images and measure attribute-based metrics between a reference and generated dataset; such an application seems relatively original to me. 
- The two first experiments demonstrate well the usefulness of the approach, able to detect a shift when the data has been curated by human, based on which attributes.

Weaknesses:
- The presentation of the paper could be improved upon. This include the writing, and the readability of the figures, as well as the quantity of information provided. This last points concerns mainly the presentation and framing of the problem of interpretability of representations, as well as the presentation and motivation of the experimental settings. 
- In particular, the paper lacks related work on concept-based representation for interpretability of images. While building metrics dedicated to generative model seems new to me, there are based on an idea which has been explored extensively before. See for example "Concept Whitening for Interpretable Image Recognition, Chen et al, 2020". 
- The paper focuses on a narrow choice of methods to generate attributes, which, to me, should be one of the key experimental investigation of the paper. Notably, the previous literature explores using different kind of attributes, coming from existing data (for example, "Interpretable Basis Decomposition for Visual Explanation, Zhou et al, 2018") or to be learnt ("A Framework to Learn with Interpretation", Parekh et al, 2021). The authors only (very shortly) argue about the number of needed attributes.
- The toy experiments seem relevant but are very fastly presented and should be expanded upon. The remaining experiments are too short to be convincing and only focus on a handful of models.

Questions:
- Could you better formalize the notation $DCS_{\mathcal{X}}(a_{i,j})$ for joint distributions ?
- Is it necessary to center the text and images differently ? They lie in the same semantic space. Also, would it not be reasonable to be using the attributes themselves, rather than captions ?

Limitations:
- Previous distributional metrics are several times referred as relying on external models in your paper. However, the attributes that you use also rely on external models (except the USER one, of course - but in this case, the computing of DCS still relies on a captioning model). How would you address this issue ?

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: The embedding space used for calculating FID is computed with Inception V3 which is trained for image classification as the target task. This means it is more likely to capture discriminative features, raising doubts about its ability to effectively evaluate generative models. There is also a need to devise a new evaluation metric that can interpret underlying factors. This paper introduces a method called Directional CLIP Score (DCS) to properly evaluate this. The pre-trained CLIP is used as the embedding space. In particular, the paper proposes "Single attribute KL div (SaKLD)" to measure single attribute alignment and "Paired attribute KL div (PaKLD)" to measure multiple attribute alignment as new metrics. This paper provides some insightful measurement result using the proposed metrics.
reviews.strengths: The effort to evaluate generative models from various perspectives through the introduction of such metrics can be considered novel and a contribution. reviews.weaknesses:
It appears to be an intrinsic limitation that a significant number of samples (50k) are still required to obtain stable results. Nonetheless, thanks to the reported findings, we can gain more insights, and I appreciate that.

reviews.questions:
1) In lines 96-97, it seems that the authors of the referenced paper already propose using the CLIP embedding space for evaluating generative model? If not, it would be helpful to clarify the differences compared to previously proposed methods. Regarding the for explainable evaluation in the related work section, it is necessary to determine whether this paper is the first to come up with it. If there are any additional previous works, it would be helpful to share the, 

2) The major concern raised is the reliance on the embedding space. What if there are biases in the CLIP space itself? For example, if being female and wearing makeup are highly correlated, even a woman who does not actually wear makeup may show high similarity to makeup attribute. In such cases, it becomes difficult to consider CLIP score or CLIP direction score as accurate measures of similarity. While a more disentangled multimodal latent space may help alleviate this problem, I'm curious about the authors' perspective on this issue.

3) Directional CLIP Score adopts a method of computing the center of training images and normalizing them. Does this mean that anyone who wants to evaluate a generative model needs to share all these training images? Also, considering the introduction of the auxiliary image captioning model called BLIP, the improvement in performance seems minimal. Do the authors believe it is worth the trade-off?

4) Question about Table 5: cI may have missed it, but was there an explanation for the last column of FID_clip in Table 5? What does it represent? Also, in Table 5, it seems that SaKLD is calculated for a single attribute. How is it computed?

5) Can PaKLD be proposed for more than two attributes? It would be great if judgments could be made for a wide range of attributes as shown in Figure 1 (b).

6) How should we interpret numerical values itself of the scores?  Although it may not be as interpretable as accuracy I'm still curious about the authors' insights based on their experience.
reviews.limitations: It seems that there are no particular specific limitations.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The embedding space used for calculating FID is computed with Inception V3 which is trained for image classification as the target task. This means it is more likely to capture discriminative features, raising doubts about its ability to effectively evaluate generative models. There is also a need to devise a new evaluation metric that can interpret underlying factors. This paper introduces a method called Directional CLIP Score (DCS) to properly evaluate this. The pre-trained CLIP is used as the embedding space. In particular, the paper proposes "Single attribute KL div (SaKLD)" to measure single attribute alignment and "Paired attribute KL div (PaKLD)" to measure multiple attribute alignment as new metrics. This paper provides some insightful measurement result using the proposed metrics.

Strengths:
The effort to evaluate generative models from various perspectives through the introduction of such metrics can be considered novel and a contribution. Especially considering the current issues such as the bias in stable diffusion [1], the proposal of such metrics can bring benefits to the field from the perspective of trustworthy AI.

Weaknesses:
It appears to be an intrinsic limitation that a significant number of samples (50k) are still required to obtain stable results. Nonetheless, thanks to the reported findings, we can gain more insights, and I appreciate that. 
Remaining concerns are written in [Questions] section.

Questions:
1) In lines 96-97, it seems that the authors of the referenced paper already propose using the CLIP embedding space for evaluating generative model? If not, it would be helpful to clarify the differences compared to previously proposed methods. Regarding the for explainable evaluation in the related work section, it is necessary to determine whether this paper is the first to come up with it. If there are any additional previous works, it would be helpful to share the, 

2) The major concern raised is the reliance on the embedding space. What if there are biases in the CLIP space itself? For example, if being female and wearing makeup are highly correlated, even a woman who does not actually wear makeup may show high similarity to makeup attribute. In such cases, it becomes difficult to consider CLIP score or CLIP direction score as accurate measures of similarity. While a more disentangled multimodal latent space may help alleviate this problem, I'm curious about the authors' perspective on this issue.

3) Directional CLIP Score adopts a method of computing the center of training images and normalizing them. Does this mean that anyone who wants to evaluate a generative model needs to share all these training images? Also, considering the introduction of the auxiliary image captioning model called BLIP, the improvement in performance seems minimal. Do the authors believe it is worth the trade-off?

4) Question about Table 5: cI may have missed it, but was there an explanation for the last column of FID_clip in Table 5? What does it represent? Also, in Table 5, it seems that SaKLD is calculated for a single attribute. How is it computed?

5) Can PaKLD be proposed for more than two attributes? It would be great if judgments could be made for a wide range of attributes as shown in Figure 1 (b).

6) How should we interpret numerical values itself of the scores?  Although it may not be as interpretable as accuracy I'm still curious about the authors' insights based on their experience.

Limitations:
It seems that there are no particular specific limitations.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
### Expanded Review  

**Summary:**  
The manuscript addresses a critical gap in generative model evaluation by proposing interpretable, attribute-based metrics. Current methods like Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) rely on embeddings from task-specific models (e.g., Inception V3 for classification), which prioritize discriminative features over generative quality and offer limited interpretability (Lines 19–35, 60–68). This work introduces a novel embedding space using CLIP, refined via **Directional CLIP Score (DCS)**, which centers embeddings around dataset-specific means (CX and CT) to enhance attribute sensitivity (Section 3.2, Eqs 2–4). The authors then propose two metrics: **Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD)** to evaluate alignment of individual attribute distributions (e.g., "glasses" frequency; Section 4.1), and **Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD)** to assess attribute correlations (e.g., "male" and "beard"; Section 4.2). Experiments validate these metrics on GANs and diffusion models, revealing dataset-dependent performance trends (e.g., diffusion models excel on FFHQ but lag on MetFaces; Table 3). While the approach advances explainability, it inherits biases from CLIP and requires large sample sizes (50k) for stability (Figure 5a), which may limit accessibility.  

**Strengths:**  
The paper’s core contribution—**attribute-driven interpretability**—addresses a significant limitation of existing metrics (e.g., FID, Precision/Recall), which fail to explain *why* a model underperforms (Lines 50–59, 100–108). By quantifying attribute-level fidelity (SaKLD) and relational integrity (PaKLD), the metrics offer actionable insights, such as identifying color biases in diffusion models (Table 4) or spurious correlations in StyleGAN2-ADA (Table 3). This aligns with emerging needs in trustworthy AI, particularly for auditing biases (e.g., gender stereotypes in Figure 1). The **methodological rigor** is evident in the design of DCS, which improves attribute discrimination over CLIPScore (Table 1, Figure 3), and the ablation studies on sample/attribute counts (Figure 5). The **empirical scope** is commendable, benchmarking diverse models (StyleGAN variants, iDDPM) across datasets (FFHQ, LSUN Cat, MetFaces), revealing nuanced trade-offs (e.g., GANs outperform diffusion models on smaller datasets; Table 3).  

**Weaknesses:**  
The requirement for **large sample sizes (≥50k images)** to stabilize SaKLD/PaKLD (Figure 5a) poses practical barriers for low-resource settings. While the authors note this (Section 5.4), they underemphasize computational costs, especially given the reliance on BLIP for attribute extraction and CLIP/DCS calculations. Additionally, the **minimal gains from BLIP-based normalization** (DCS vs. CLIPScore accuracy in Table 1) raise questions about complexity trade-offs. The **narrow attribute focus** (e.g., facial features in CelebA) limits applicability to domains with ambiguous attributes (e.g., abstract art; Section 6), though this is acknowledged.  

**Questions:**  
1. **CLIP Embedding Prior Work**: Kynkäänniemi et al. (2022, Line 96) propose CLIP for FID to mitigate dataset biases but do not address *interpretability*. This work distinctively leverages CLIP for *attribute-level* explanations via DCS and joint distribution metrics (SaKLD/PaKLD), advancing beyond mere distance measurement (Sections 3–4).  
2. **CLIP Bias Propagation**: If CLIP embeddings encode spurious correlations (e.g., "female" and "makeup"), DCS could inherit these, misrepresenting attribute independence. The paper does not test this; experiments use curated attributes (e.g., CelebA annotations) without stress-testing entangled concepts. A discussion of this risk (e.g., adversarial attribute pairs) is needed.  
3. **Training Data Dependency**: Computing CX/CT requires *full training data* (Eq 2), which conflicts with privacy-sensitive domains (e.g., medical images). BLIP’s marginal utility (Table 1) warrants scrutiny: if user-defined attributes suffice (Section 3.3), is BLIP’s overhead justified?  
4. **Metric Interpretation**: SaKLD aggregates KL divergences for each attribute’s DCS distribution (Eq 5), but Table 2 reports it as a scalar. Clarify if this is averaged across attributes. For FID_CLIP (Table 2), it measures the 2-Wasserstein distance using CLIP embeddings (Line 96), but its role alongside SaKLD/PaKLD needs elaboration.  
5. **PaKLD Scalability**: The metric evaluates pairwise attribute correlations (Eq 6) but cannot scale to higher-order interactions (e.g., triples like "age × gender × glasses"). The authors should discuss this limitation relative to Figure 1’s vision of multi-attribute explanations.  
6. **Score Calibration**: SaKLD/PaKLD values (e.g., 9.902 for StyleGAN1 in Table 3) lack intuitive baselines. While lower is better, context is needed (e.g., is 5.0 "good"?). The paper would benefit from human-evaluated alignment (e.g., correlating PaKLD spikes with perceptual biases).  

**Limitations:**  
The approach assumes **semantically meaningful attributes** are extractable, which fails for non-figurative data (e.g., fractals; Section 6). It also **inherits CLIP’s biases** (e.g., Western-centric attributes from training data), potentially skewing evaluations. While briefly noted, these deserve deeper mitigation strategies.  

**Rating:**  
**6: Weak Accept**  
The paper introduces a technically sound framework for explainable evaluation, validated through rigorous experiments. Its impact is moderate-to-high: attribute-level insights could become standard tools for model auditing. Weaknesses (sample size, biases) are offset by novelty and empirical depth. Recommendations:  
- Address CLIP bias propagation (Question 2).  
- Clarify metric aggregation (Question 4).  
- Discuss higher-order attribute extensions (Question 5).

Summary:
The embedding space used for calculating FID is computed with Inception V3 which is trained for image classification as the target task. This means it is more likely to capture discriminative features, raising doubts about its ability to effectively evaluate generative models. There is also a need to devise a new evaluation metric that can interpret underlying factors. This paper introduces a method called Directional CLIP Score (DCS) to properly evaluate this. The pre-trained CLIP is used as the embedding space. In particular, the paper proposes "Single attribute KL div (SaKLD)" to measure single attribute alignment and "Paired attribute KL div (PaKLD)" to measure multiple attribute alignment as new metrics. This paper provides some insightful measurement result using the proposed metrics.

Strengths:
The effort to evaluate generative models from various perspectives through the introduction of such metrics can be considered novel and a contribution. Especially considering the current issues such as the bias in stable diffusion [1], the proposal of such metrics can bring benefits to the field from the perspective of trustworthy AI.

Weaknesses:
It appears to be an intrinsic limitation that a significant number of samples (50k) are still required to obtain stable results. Nonetheless, thanks to the reported findings, we can gain more insights, and I appreciate that. 
Remaining concerns are written in [Questions] section.

Regarding the for explainable evaluation in the related work section, it is necessary to determine whether this paper is the first to come up with it. reviews.summary: This is a very interesting research work. The main contribution of this paper is to consider the attribute information in the original training data when evaluating the quality of images generated by the model. There are two benefits to this approach: 1) determining whether the model can correctly imitate the distribution of the training data; 2) explaining which attributes the model does not perform well on. In implementing this idea, the authors found that directly calculating the CLIPScore between the image representation and the text representation of the attribute does not yield distinctive results. Therefore, they proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC). The main idea of this approach is to move the reference point for calculating vector similarity to a more reasonable point. At the same time, they proposed two methods to apply DSC, one is Single attribute KL Divergence, and the other is Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) considering the combination of attributes.
reviews.strengths:
1. I think this research question is very interesting. It is very meaningful to use the attribute information of the dataset to help evaluate the quality of the model's generation.
2. The Directional CLIPScore (DSC) proposed by the authors is very concise and appears to be quite effective in the case study.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. In Section 3.3, I agree with the extraction of attributes using the BLIP and manual annotation methods. However, one method of extracting attributes is to generate an attribute list with GPT and then filter it with training data. I think the attribute list generated by GPT in advance may bring biases. The core of this paper is mainly to study the correlation between the model and the training data. However, if the list generated by GPT is not the most representative attribute, the results may be biased.
2. The experimental part in Sections 5.1-5.3 does not seem very convincing. The authors mainly verify that the proposed method can indeed be consistent with some expected experimental designs, but there is a lack of more convincing quantitative indicators to show that their proposed evaluation metrics are better than those proposed by others previous research works. I would prefer to see the authors analyze the correlation coefficient between their proposed evaluation metrics and human evaluation, as well as whether their evaluation metrics have improved in terms of correlation coefficient compared to previous evaluation indicators. This is my biggest concern for this work.
reviews.questions:
1. In Section 3.1, can the problem exhibited by CLIPScore be solved by directly calculating the Euclidean distance between E(x) and E(a) without relying on Directional CLIPScore?
2. Notation overload. The meanings of N used in Eq. (5) and Eq. (2) are different.
reviews.limitations: see weakness
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This is a very interesting research work. The main contribution of this paper is to consider the attribute information in the original training data when evaluating the quality of images generated by the model. There are two benefits to this approach: 1) determining whether the model can correctly imitate the distribution of the training data; 2) explaining which attributes the model does not perform well on. In implementing this idea, the authors found that directly calculating the CLIPScore between the image representation and the text representation of the attribute does not yield distinctive results. Therefore, they proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC). The main idea of this approach is to move the reference point for calculating vector similarity to a more reasonable point. At the same time, they proposed two methods to apply DSC, one is Single attribute KL Divergence, and the other is Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) considering the combination of attributes.

Strengths:
1. I think this research question is very interesting. It is very meaningful to use the attribute information of the dataset to help evaluate the quality of the model's generation.
2. The Directional CLIPScore (DSC) proposed by the authors is very concise and appears to be quite effective in the case study.

Weaknesses:
1. In Section 3.3, I agree with the extraction of attributes using the BLIP and manual annotation methods. However, one method of extracting attributes is to generate an attribute list with GPT and then filter it with training data. I think the attribute list generated by GPT in advance may bring biases. The core of this paper is mainly to study the correlation between the model and the training data. However, if the list generated by GPT is not the most representative attribute, the results may be biased.
2. The experimental part in Sections 5.1-5.3 does not seem very convincing. The authors mainly verify that the proposed method can indeed be consistent with some expected experimental designs, but there is a lack of more convincing quantitative indicators to show that their proposed evaluation metrics are better than those proposed by others previous research works. I would prefer to see the authors analyze the correlation coefficient between their proposed evaluation metrics and human evaluation, as well as whether their evaluation metrics have improved in terms of correlation coefficient compared to previous evaluation indicators. This is my biggest concern for this work.

Questions:
1. In Section 3.1, can the problem exhibited by CLIPScore be solved by directly calculating the Euclidean distance between E(x) and E(a) without relying on Directional CLIPScore?
2. Notation overload. The meanings of N used in Eq. (5) and Eq. (2) are different.

Limitations:
see weakness

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
### Expanded Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper presents a compelling approach to enhancing the interpretability of generative model evaluation. The core contribution lies in leveraging attribute-specific information from training data to assess both the fidelity and diversity of generated images. By introducing Directional CLIPScore (DCS) to address the limitations of standard CLIPScore, the authors enable more discriminative attribute quantification. This foundation supports two novel metrics—Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD) and Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD)—which respectively evaluate attribute distribution alignment and attribute-pair relationships. The work is timely, as the field increasingly recognizes the inadequacy of opaque metrics like FID. However, while the methodology is innovative, the experimental validation requires strengthening to fully substantiate the claims.  

**Strengths:**  
1. **Novel Research Direction:** The focus on interpretable evaluation metrics addresses a critical gap in generative model assessment. By tying evaluation to human-understandable attributes (e.g., "gender," "eyeglasses," "smile" in CelebA), the method provides actionable insights beyond scalar scores. For instance, Figure 1 illustrates how DCS pinpoints biases (e.g., Model 2’s overrepresentation of "long hair" and "makeup"), which aligns with human intuition but eludes traditional metrics.  
2. **Elegant Technical Solution:** DCS effectively mitigates CLIPScore’s key weakness—its compressed similarity values for human-centric attributes (Section 3.1). By recentering embeddings around dataset-specific centroids (Figure 3), DCS amplifies discriminative power. Table 1 validates this, showing DCS’s superior accuracy (0.530 vs. 0.501 for refined attributes) in capturing ground-truth annotations. This innovation is both simple and impactful.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Attribute Selection Bias:** The GPT-3-driven attribute generation (Section 3.3) risks introducing external biases. GPT-3 queries (e.g., "Give me 50 words of useful visual attributes...") yield generic descriptors that may not reflect dataset-specific nuances. For example, LSUN Cat’s "color" attributes (Table 4) could be misaligned if GPT-3 prioritizes shape over texture. While BLIP and manual annotation mitigate this, the paper underanalyzes GPT-3’s impact on metric stability. A sensitivity analysis (e.g., varying attribute sets) would clarify robustness.  
2. **Insufficient Metric Validation:** The experiments (Sections 5.1–5.3) demonstrate consistency with synthetic scenarios (e.g., correlated image injections in Table 2) but lack comparison to human judgment. For instance, the CelebA-HQ toy experiment (Figure 4) shows PaKLD detecting attribute-pair discrepancies, but without correlating these results with human ratings of "realism" or "diversity," the metric’s practical utility remains unproven. Quantitative benchmarks (e.g., Spearman correlation between SaKLD/PaKLD and human evaluations across diverse datasets) are needed to position these metrics against established baselines.  

**Questions:**  
1. **Alternative Solutions to CLIPScore:** In Section 3.1, could Euclidean distance between $E_I(x)$ and $E_T(a)$ resolve CLIPScore’s narrow value distribution? While cosine similarity suffers from clustering, Euclidean distance might amplify noise. The paper should briefly discuss why directionality (DCS) is superior to distance-based fixes.  
2. **Notation Overload:** The dual use of $N$—as sample count in Eq. (2) and attribute count in Eq. (5)—creates ambiguity. For example, Section 5 uses $N=20$ attributes but $N=30,000$ images. Standardizing notation (e.g., $N_{\text{attr}}$, $N_{\text{samp}}$) would improve clarity.  

**Limitations:**  
The reliance on attribute clarity restricts the method’s applicability. Ambiguous datasets (e.g., abstract art with ill-defined attributes) or low-quality generations (Section 6) may yield unreliable DCS values. Additionally, the metric assumes attribute independence in SaKLD, though real-world correlations (e.g., "beard" and "male") are handled only in PaKLD. This could oversimplify scenarios requiring higher-order attribute interactions.  

**Recommendation:**  
This work makes a meaningful stride toward interpretable evaluation, with DCS and the KL-based metrics offering a fresh lens for model diagnosis. However, the borderline-accept rating (5) stems from the need for stronger empirical validation. Addressing the human-correlation gap and attribute-bias concerns would elevate the contribution from promising to definitive.  

---  
**Decision:**  
**5: Borderline accept** (Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation).
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This is a very interesting research work. The main contribution of this paper is to consider the attribute information in the original training data when evaluating the quality of images generated by the model. There are two benefits to this approach: 1) determining whether the model can correctly imitate the distribution of the training data; 2) explaining which attributes the model does not perform well on. In implementing this idea, the authors found that directly calculating the CLIPScore between the image representation and the text representation of the attribute does not yield distinctive results. Therefore, they proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC). The main idea of this approach is to move the reference point for calculating vector similarity to a more reasonable point. At the same time, they proposed two methods to apply DSC, one is Single attribute KL Divergence, and the other is Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) considering the combination of attributes.

Strengths:
1. I think this research question is very interesting. It is very meaningful to use the attribute information of the dataset to help evaluate the quality of the model's generation.
2. The Directional CLIPScore (DSC) proposed by the authors is very concise and appears to be quite effective in the case study.

Weaknesses:
1. In Section 3.3, I agree with the extraction of attributes using the BLIP and manual annotation methods. However, one method of extracting attributes is to generate an attribute list with GPT and then filter it with training data. I think the attribute list generated by GPT in advance may bring biases. The core of this paper is mainly to study the correlation between the model and the training data. However, if the list generated by GPT is not the most representative attribute, the results may be biased.
2. The experimental part in Sections 5.1-5.3 does not seem very convincing. The authors mainly verify that the proposed method can indeed be consistent with some expected experimental designs, but there is a lack of more convincing quantitative indicators to show that their proposed evaluation metrics are better than those proposed by others previous research works. I would prefer to see the authors analyze the correlation coefficient between their proposed evaluation metrics and human evaluation, as well as whether their evaluation metrics have improved in terms of correlation coefficient compared to previous evaluation indicators. This is my biggest concern for this work.

Questions:
1. In Section 3.1, can the problem exhibited by CLIPScore be solved by directly calculating the Euclidean distance between E(x) and E(a) without relying on Directional CLIPScore?
2. Notation overload. The meanings of N used in Eq. (5) and Eq. (2) are different.

Limitations:
see weakness

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new metric to evaluate the quality and diversity of generated images based on
interpretable embeddings. To obtain the interpretable embeddings for selected attributes, the cluster
centers of the encodings of two separate encoders, one for images and the other for the text attributes
are calculated and the interpretable embeddings are the difference between the encodings of each
image and attribute and their respective cluster centers. The interpretable embeddings represent the
direction in which a particular embedding lies with respect to it’s cluster center. The CLIPScore
between the interpretable embeddings of an image and an attribute is calculated and is named as
’Directional CLIPScore’, since the interpretable embeddings represent the ’direction’.
The authors have proposed two metrics : 1. SaKLD - to quatify how closely the attribute distri-
bution in generated images matches with that of training images. 2. PaKLD - to quantify correlations
between different attributes. The KL divergence between the probability density functions of the
Directional CLIPScores for all the images in the train data and generated data.
Using SaKLD, the KL divergence between the attribute distribution in training and generated
images is calculated. PaKLD calculates the KL divergence similar to SaKLD, except that the presence
of a pair of attributes is required in the training and generated images.
reviews.strengths: The motivation for the idea is good and has a huge potential impact for improving evaluation of generative models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although, the motivation for developing this metric is valid, the overall methodology and the
experimental results are not convincing for the use of this metric. Also, the experiments performed
are inadequate and do not sufficiently justify how well the metric performs compared to the previously
proposed metrics. Additionally, previous metrics can directly evaluate quality of generation based
on the generated images alone, but this metric heavily relies on the attributes in the form of text
descriptions. Thus, it limits the applicability and generalizability of this metric.
- The proposed metrics use CLIPScore (which already exists) for interpretable embeddings and then
applies KL Divergence for the PDFs of the ClipScores of images and attributes, thus, showing limited
novelty.
- Most of the paper is easy to follow but some important parts like, how is the center of text attributes
calculated, results from table 1 (what does accuracy stand for) etc. are a bit ambiguous. There is a
lot of scope to improve the technical soundness of the paper. Although some of the popular metrics
1are mentioned, there has been a lot of work in the generative modelling domain which the literature
survey must cover. The proposed methodology is not very sound and is not well supported with the
experimental setup. The results are also not sufficiently explained. Diversity is the main motivation
for the paper as it is mentioned in the abstract but any theoretical or empirical work to support it is
completely missing.
- Motivation and methodology including the steps to evaluate generated samples is understandable but
some parts are ambiguous (please refer above comments).
- Based on the current state of the experiments, the contributions don’t seem to be significant as there
is not enough validation to support the claims.

reviews.questions:
1. If I understood correctly, the text and the image encoders are different? If yes, then
the embedding space of both models is going to be very different and will be influenced by the
downstream tasks that they are trained on. Would this metric always work for embeddings from
different models? Does it make sense to calculate similarity between embeddings from different
models?
2. Table 1 represents accuracy results for CS vs DCS. What is meant by evaluating ”positive
samples” in line 164 and 165? And what is meant by ”how well they are aligned”? Also, what
exactly is the accuracy calculated for?
3. In Section 3.1, there is a mention of ”each channel of the embedding being utilized”, how exactly
does this happen?
4. Line 196-197 claims that the generated distribution is desirable only if it has exactly same number
of samples as in the training set that has a particular attribute. Why should it be? Afaik, the
underlying distribution of latents represent semantic features of the data and the generative
model is responsible to generate novel samples that could be realistic but not necessarily show
exactly same features same number of times in the training set. So, is the assumption in the
mentioned lines appropriate? Can you prove this or maybe refer to some papers that provide
such guarantees?
reviews.limitations: limitations have been addressed

Summary:
This paper proposes a new metric to evaluate the quality and diversity of generated images based on
interpretable embeddings. To obtain the interpretable embeddings for selected attributes, the cluster
centers of the encodings of two separate encoders, one for images and the other for the text attributes
are calculated and the interpretable embeddings are the difference between the encodings of each
image and attribute and their respective cluster centers. The interpretable embeddings represent the
direction in which a particular embedding lies with respect to it’s cluster center. The CLIPScore
between the interpretable embeddings of an image and an attribute is calculated and is named as

The authors have proposed two metrics : 1. SaKLD - to quatify how closely the attribute distri-
bution in generated images matches with that of training images. 2. PaKLD - to quantify correlations
between different attributes. The KL divergence between the probability density functions of the
Directional CLIPScores for all the images in the train data and generated data.
Using SaKLD, the KL divergence between the attribute distribution in training and generated
images is calculated. PaKLD calculates the KL divergence similar to SaKLD, except that the presence
of a pair of attributes is required in the training and generated images.

Strengths:
The motivation for the idea is good and has a huge potential impact for improving evaluation of generative models.

Weaknesses:
- Although, the motivation for developing this metric is valid, the overall methodology and the
experimental results are not convincing for the use of this metric. Also, the experiments performed
are inadequate and do not sufficiently justify how well the metric performs compared to the previously
proposed metrics. Additionally, previous metrics can directly evaluate quality of generation based
on the generated images alone, but this metric heavily relies on the attributes in the form of text
descriptions. Thus, it limits the applicability and generalizability of this metric.
- The proposed metrics use CLIPScore (which already exists) for interpretable embeddings and then
applies KL Divergence for the PDFs of the ClipScores of images and attributes, thus, showing limited
novelty.

calculated, results from table 1 (what does accuracy stand for) etc. are a bit ambiguous. There is a
lot of scope to improve the technical soundness of the paper. Although some of the popular metrics
1are mentioned, there has been a lot of work in the generative modelling domain which the literature
survey must cover. The proposed methodology is not very sound and is not well supported with the
experimental setup. The results are also not sufficiently explained. Diversity is the main motivation
for the paper as it is mentioned in the abstract but any theoretical or empirical work to support it is
completely missing.

some parts are ambiguous (please refer above comments).
- Based on the current state of the experiments, the contributions don’t seem to be significant as there
is not enough validation to support the claims.

reviews.text:
### Expanded Peer Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a novel evaluation framework for generative models using interpretable, attribute-driven embeddings. The core innovation involves transforming deep embeddings (e.g., CLIP) into attribute-aligned representations via directional offsets from dataset-specific centers. This enables two new metrics: (1) **SaKLD**, which quantifies distributional mismatches in single attributes (e.g., "glasses" frequency), and (2) **PaKLD**, which evaluates correlations between attribute pairs (e.g., "beard" and "woman"). The authors position these metrics as tools for diagnosing diversity failures (e.g., bias toward "long hair" in Figure 1). While the motivation—addressing the "black-box" nature of metrics like FID—is compelling, the methodology and validation exhibit significant limitations that undermine the paper's contributions.  

---

### Strengths:  
**Original Motivation and Potential Impact:** The focus on interpretability addresses a critical gap in generative model evaluation. Metrics like FID collapse quality/diversity into a single score, obscuring *why* a model underperforms (e.g., mode collapse in specific attributes). The proposed framework could empower practitioners to diagnose and rectify biases, particularly in societally sensitive domains (e.g., facial generation). Figure 1 effectively illustrates this gap: existing metrics detect Model 2’s inferiority but lack explanatory power, while the attribute-based approach pinpoints "long hair" and "makeup" as biases.  

---

### Weaknesses:  
**1. Limited Novelty in Core Methodology:**  
The core technique—**Directional CLIPScore (DCS)**—builds directly on CLIPScore (Hessel et al., 2021), merely shifting the similarity calculation away from the origin to dataset-specific centers (Sec. 3.2). While this improves attribute discrimination (Table 1), it is an incremental adjustment rather than a conceptual leap. Similarly, **SaKLD** and **PaKLD** apply KL divergence to attribute distributions, a well-established statistical approach. The manuscript does not adequately distinguish these metrics from prior distributional comparisons (e.g., FID’s use of Gaussian divergences). The primary novelty lies in the *interpretability* of the embeddings, but the paper does not rigorously establish how DCS surpasses simpler attribute-classification baselines.  

**2. Inadequate Experimental Validation:**  
- **Lack of Comparative Analysis:** The experiments (Sec. 5) validate the metrics’ sensitivity to curated biases (e.g., injecting "man with makeup" images) but fail to compare against existing metrics beyond FID. For instance, Precision/Recall (Sajjadi et al.) or Density/Coverage (Naeem et al.) also detect diversity issues but are not benchmarked. The claim that DCS-based metrics offer "superior sensitivity" (Table 2) is unsupported without this comparison.  
- **Unsubstantiated Model Evaluations:** Table 3 reports SaKLD/PaKLD scores for StyleGAN and diffusion models but offers no insight into *why* iDDPM underperforms on color attributes. The hypothesis that diffusion models prioritize "texture/color-level similarity" (Sec. 5.3) is speculative and lacks empirical validation (e.g., via attribute disentanglement studies).  
- **Toy Experiments Dominate:** The "necessity of PaKLD" experiment (Fig. 4) uses a synthetic CelebA-HQ subset (20k "smiling men" vs. "non-smiling women"). While illustrative, it does not reflect real-world generative failures. Broader validation on standard benchmarks (e.g., FFHQ, LSUN) with *natural* model biases is missing.  

**3. Technical Ambiguities and Oversights:**  
- **Attribute Selection Vagueness:** Section 3.3 proposes three attribute-selection methods (BLIP, user annotation, GPT-3) but does not evaluate their impact on metric stability. For example, in Table 2, "GPT attributes" yield lower PaKLD scores than "BLIP," suggesting metric sensitivity to attribute choice—yet this is unexplored.  
- **Unclear Embedding Alignment:** The image and text encoders ($E_I$, $E_T$) are trained independently (CLIP), operating in non-identical embedding spaces. Calculating similarity between their directional offsets ($V_x$, $V_a$) assumes these spaces are geometrically aligned, which is not theoretically justified (Sec. 3.2).  
- **Accuracy Metric Ambiguity:** Table 1 claims DCS improves "mean accuracy" over CLIPScore but omits details: What task defines "accuracy"? How were positive/negative samples defined? Without this, the results are uninterpretable.  

**4. Questionable Assumptions:**  
- **Overemphasis on Exact Attribute Frequencies:** SaKLD assumes a "desirable" generator must replicate training-set attribute counts exactly (Sec. 4.1: "the model should generate exactly 3,000 images with eyeglasses"). This contradicts generative modeling principles, where the goal is distributional similarity—not point estimates. A model generating 2,900 "eyeglasses" samples could still be high-quality if variance is preserved.  
- **Attribute Reliance Limits Applicability:** The method is restricted to domains with well-defined attributes (e.g., faces). For complex or abstract data (e.g., "modern art" in Sec. 6), attribute annotation is infeasible, rendering the metrics unusable. By contrast, FID requires no such supervision.  

**5. Presentational Issues:**  
- **Figure/Text Inconsistencies:** Figure 1(b) is referenced as evidence for metric interpretability (line 43), but the text mislabels it as "Figure 1(a)." Figure 2 and Table 1 are never discussed in the main text, weakening their relevance.  
- **Contradictory Captions:** Figure 4’s caption describes generated images as "non-smiling men and smiling women," but Sec. 5.2 states they are "non-smiling men and non-smiling women"—a critical discrepancy for interpreting PaKLD.  
- **Unsubstantiated Claims:** Lines 56–58 assert that missing attributes (e.g., "gender, age") preclude "visually realistic images," but StyleGAN-generated faces lacking attributes can still be photorealistic.  

---

### Additional Questions:  
1. **Embedding Space Misalignment:** If $E_I$ (image) and $E_T$ (text) produce embeddings in different CLIP subspaces, does projecting their offsets ($V_x$, $V_a$) into a shared space introduce distortion? How does this affect metric reliability?  
2. **Sensitivity to Dataset Shifts:** How do SaKLD/PaKLD respond to training data with evolving attributes (e.g., new "hairstyles" in FFHQ over time)? The reliance on fixed attributes ($A$) may limit robustness.  
3. **Computational Cost:** Calculating pairwise attribute joint distributions (PaKLD) for $M = \binom{N}{2}$ pairs scales quadratically with attributes. How does this overhead compare to FID?  

---

### Conclusion:  
While the paper identifies a legitimate need for interpretability in generative evaluation, the methodology relies on incremental adaptations of existing tools (CLIPScore, KL divergence) without sufficient innovation. The experiments lack comparative rigor, and technical ambiguities—especially regarding attribute selection and embedding alignment—undermine the proposed metrics' reliability. The framework is also narrowly applicable to attribute-rich domains. For these reasons, the contributions do not yet meet the bar for acceptance. **Significant revisions**—including broader validation, ablation studies, and theoretical grounding—are needed to establish viability.  

**Rating:**  
**4: Borderline Reject**  
*Justification:* Technically sound motivation but outweighed by limited novelty, inadequate evaluation, and unresolved methodological flaws.  

---  
**Typos/Edits:**  
- Sec. 3.3 heading: "attribute selection methodologies" → "Attribute Selection Methodologies"  
- Line 43: "Figure 1 (b)" → "Figure 1 (a)"  
- Line 147: Add reference to Figure 3.  
- Line 253/Fig. 4: Clarify whether generated images contain "smiling" women.

title: Residual Scheduling: A New Reinforcement Learning Approach to Solving Job Shop Scheduling Problem

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a deep reinforcement learning-based constructive heuristic to solve the (Flexible) Job Shop Scheduling Problem. An instance of the problem is represented as a graph and fed into a Graph Neural Network-based model which outputs a score for each candidate (operation-machine) pair. The model is trained with the REINFORCE algorithm with as baseline a classic Priority Dispatching Rule-based heuristic. The novelty of the paper lies in the update of the state after each action: the graph is updated by removing the operations which have already been executed to focus on the most relevant information, which is the residual operations and remaining times of the ongoing ones. The proposed approach is experimentally shown to outperform RL-based constructive heuristics on classic JSSP and FJSSP benchmarks.
reviews.strengths:
1. Sound and interesting idea of removing irrelevant information from the state 
1. The paper is fairly clear and I appreciated the illustrations Fig 1-3.
1. The model was proposed for the JSSP and easily adapted to the Flexible JSSP
1. The approach outperforms deep RL-based construction heuristics on classic benchmarks
reviews.weaknesses:
1. Limited novelty: the main contribution is the definition of the residual state at each step of the construction process by removing irrelevant operations and resetting the time reference. This seems to me an incremental improvement of the approach L2D [1], which already proposed a similar state graph representation and the use of the Graph Isomorphism Network architecture for the JSSP.
1. The proposed approach seems very specific to (F)JSSPs (state representation, baseline) and it's not clear what could be transferable to DRL heuristics for solving other optimization problems.
1. In the experiments, the presented non-learning-based baselines seem pretty weak: only greedy (see more in Questions)
1. Lots of English typos (I noted a few per page)

[1] C Zhang et al, Learning to Dispatch for Job Shop Scheduling via Deep Reinforcement Learning, Neurips 2020
reviews.questions:
1. Where dos the attributes of edges (L205) appear in the GNN model (Sec 3.3)?
1. The paper claims: “Interestingly, using RS, the average gaps are nearly zero for the collections with sizes larger than 100 … A strong implication is that our RS approach can be scaled up for job sizes and even reach the optimal for sufficient large job count.” Do you have an idea of why the model would work better on unseen large instances versus instances of the same size as the training ones? Does OR-tools return the optimal solutions for these larger instances? Can it be that the quality of the reference solutions decreases and therefore the “optimality” gap becomes smaller?
1. Are there stronger non-learning-based baselines for the JSPP other than the greedy PDR heuristics presented in Table 2? To be able to appreciate the performance of the proposed approach, it would be useful and more convincing to compare it to the best heuristics for this problem, beyond simple greedy ones. For example maybe [2]?
1. Appendix, Algorithm 1, Line 6:  to compute this makespan at state s_t, given action a_t, do you do a rollout of the current policy \pi_{\theta} with the updated parameter \theta? This would mean doing a rollout until the end of both the baseline and current policy at each step of the trajectory? How long did the training take?
1. How is the average computation time computed? (Table 13) In particular, was the policy applied to each instance individually or were instances batched? 
1. The discussion L322 about the time it takes for RS/L2D/ScheduleNet versus OR-Tools can be a bit misleading: OR-tools is an exact solver. If spends a lot of time proving the optimality of the solution. Probably it could return a good quality solution much faster if used as a heuristic. In addition it runs on CPUs and not GPUs therefore just comparing the time is not really fair. 

[2] CY Zhang, A tabu search algorithm with a new neighborhood structure for the job shop scheduling problem, Computers & Operations Research, 2007
reviews.limitations: Not explicitly addressed by the paper.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.

1. The paper is fairly clear and I appreciated the illustrations Fig 1-3.

1. The approach outperforms deep RL-based construction heuristics on classic benchmarks

Weaknesses:
1. Limited novelty: the main contribution is the definition of the residual state at each step of the construction process by removing irrelevant operations and resetting the time reference. This seems to me an incremental improvement of the approach L2D [1], which already proposed a similar state graph representation and the use of the Graph Isomorphism Network architecture for the JSSP.
1. The proposed approach seems very specific to (F)JSSPs (state representation, baseline) and it's not clear what could be transferable to DRL heuristics for solving other optimization problems.
1. In the experiments, the presented non-learning-based baselines seem pretty weak: only greedy (see more in Questions)
1. Lots of English typos (I noted a few per page)

[1] C Zhang et al, Learning to Dispatch for Job Shop Scheduling via Deep Reinforcement Learning, Neurips 2020

Questions:
1. Where dos the attributes of edges (L205) appear in the GNN model (Sec 3.3)?
1. The paper claims: “Interestingly, using RS, the average gaps are nearly zero for the collections with sizes larger than 100 … A strong implication is that our RS approach can be scaled up for job sizes and even reach the optimal for sufficient large job count.” Do you have an idea of why the model would work better on unseen large instances versus instances of the same size as the training ones? Does OR-tools return the optimal solutions for these larger instances? Can it be that the quality of the reference solutions decreases and therefore the “optimality” gap becomes smaller?
1. Are there stronger non-learning-based baselines for the JSPP other than the greedy PDR heuristics presented in Table 2? To be able to appreciate the performance of the proposed approach, it would be useful and more convincing to compare it to the best heuristics for this problem, beyond simple greedy ones. For example maybe [2]?
1. Appendix, Algorithm 1, Line 6:  to compute this makespan at state s_t, given action a_t, do you do a rollout of the current policy \pi_{\theta} with the updated parameter \theta? This would mean doing a rollout until the end of both the baseline and current policy at each step of the trajectory? How long did the training take?
1. How is the average computation time computed? (Table 13) In particular, was the policy applied to each instance individually or were instances batched? 
1. The discussion L322 about the time it takes for RS/L2D/ScheduleNet versus OR-Tools can be a bit misleading: OR-tools is an exact solver. If spends a lot of time proving the optimality of the solution. Probably it could return a good quality solution much faster if used as a heuristic. In addition it runs on CPUs and not GPUs therefore just comparing the time is not really fair. 

[2] CY Zhang, A tabu search algorithm with a new neighborhood structure for the job shop scheduling problem, Computers & Operations Research, 2007

Limitations:
Not explicitly addressed by the paper.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
### Summary:
This paper introduces a novel deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based constructive heuristic for solving the Job Shop Scheduling Problem (JSP) and Flexible Job Shop Scheduling Problem (FJSP). The approach represents scheduling states as graphs where irrelevant completed operations are dynamically pruned ("residual scheduling"), focusing the model on pending operations and machines. These residual graphs are processed through a heterogeneous Graph Isomorphism Network (HGIN) to score candidate (operation-machine) pairs, with actions selected via greedy policy. The model is trained using REINFORCE with a priority dispatching rule (MWKR) as baseline. Experimental results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on standard JSP/FJSP benchmarks, with particularly strong scalability to large instances (e.g., zero gap on 49/50 instances with >150 jobs). The residual state formulation is presented as the core innovation, though the overall architecture builds upon prior GNN-based scheduling work.

### Strengths:
1. **Residual State Formulation:** The concept of dynamically pruning completed operations and resetting the temporal context (Section 3.1) is well-motivated and intuitively aligns with human scheduling intuition. The manuscript provides clear examples (Figure 2) showing how ongoing operations are modified to reflect remaining processing times, effectively reducing state complexity. This innovation is validated through strong ablation studies (RS vs. RS+op in Tables 2-3).  
2. **Clarity and Presentation:** The paper is logically structured, with particularly effective visualizations (Figures 1-3) that clarify JSP/FJSP mechanics, state transitions, and model architecture. The problem formulation (Section 2) succinctly establishes necessary context, and the residual scheduling paradigm is rigorously defined.  
3. **Flexibility Across Problem Variants:** The architecture seamlessly extends from JSP to FJSP (Section 4.3) by incorporating averaged processing times for multi-machine operations (Section 3.2). This adaptability is evidenced by consistent SOTA results on both problem types (Tables 2-3).  
4. **Empirical Performance:** Comprehensive benchmarking across TA/ABZ/FT/ORB/YN/SWV/LA datasets (Section 4.2, Appendix) demonstrates unambiguous superiority over existing DRL heuristics (L2D, ScheduleNet). The scalability results (Section 5, Figure 4) are compelling—especially the near-zero gaps for large instances (n>100), suggesting exceptional generalization despite training on small (n≤10) problems.  

### Weaknesses:
1. **Limited Novelty in Core Architecture:** While residual state updates are novel, the overall framework heavily leverages established components: graph representations mirror Park et al. (2021a), HGIN derives from Zhang et al. (2020), and REINFORCE training is standard. The paper positions itself as an incremental improvement over L2D/ScheduleNet rather than a paradigm shift.  
2. **Problem-Specific Design:** Key elements—job/machine attributes (Section 3.2), edge definitions (O→O, M→O), and the MWKR baseline—are tightly coupled with JSP/FJSP structure. The paper does not discuss how residual scheduling might generalize to other DRL-based combinatorial optimization (e.g., vehicle routing or bin packing).  
3. **Weak Non-Learning Baselines:** Comparisons are limited to simple PDR heuristics (MWKR, FIFO, SPT) and DRL methods. Omitting state-of-the-art metaheuristics like tabu search (Dell’Amico & Trubian, 1993) or hybrid GA/TS methods (Zhang, 2007) makes it difficult to contextualize absolute solution quality. For instance, the 0.232 gap on MK (Table 3) is impressive vs. PDRs but may trail specialized non-learning solvers.  

### Questions:
1. **Edge Attributes in GNN:** Section 3.2 defines normalized processing times for edges (¯T(edge)), but Section 3.3’s HGIN equations (Eq 2-3) only reference node embeddings. How are edge features incorporated? Are they concatenated to node features or used in message-passing?  
2. **Zero-Gap Phenomenon in Large Instances:** The near-optimal performance for n>100 (Section 5) is intriguing. Is this because:  
   - OR-Tools’ reference solutions degrade for large instances (as optimality becomes harder to prove)?  
   - Residual states disproportionately benefit large n by eliminating redundant information?  
   - The problem structure of Taillard instances favors the model’s inductive biases?  
3. **Stronger Non-Learning Baselines:** Why not compare against established methods like shifting bottleneck (Adams et al., 1988) or tabu search (Zhang, 2007)? These are standard in JSP literature and would better anchor the empirical advances.  
4. **Training Computational Cost:** Algorithm 1 (Appendix) requires computing T(mksp)_π(s_t, a_t) via rollouts. Does this entail simulating full trajectories under the current policy for every action? If so, how was training time managed (e.g., distributed rollouts)? Table 13 notes ~30s inference but omits training costs.  
5. **Computation Time Measurement:** Table 13 reports "average computation times" for inference. Were instances solved sequentially, or did batching exploit GPU parallelism? The latter could artificially deflate wall-clock time.  
6. **OR-Tools Comparison Fairness:** Contrasting GPU-accelerated DRL inference (30s) against CPU-based OR-Tools (4,000s) is misleading. Did you evaluate OR-Tools with early termination (e.g., 30s) to compare solution quality under equal time?  

### Limitations:
The paper does not explicitly discuss limitations, but several arise from the methodology:  
- **Dependence on Reference Solutions:** Gap calculations (Eq 6) rely on OR-Tools, which cannot guarantee optimality for large instances. Suboptimal T(mksp)_π∗ inflates perceived performance.  
- **Training Complexity:** REINFORCE with rollouts is sample-inefficient. Training "1 million episodes" (Section 4.1) suggests high computational overhead, though specifics are omitted.  
- **Generalization Scope:** Success hinges on Taillard-style instances. Performance on radically different distributions (e.g., non-uniform machine workloads) is untested.  

### Rating:  
**6: Weak Accept**  
The residual scheduling approach is technically sound and delivers measurable improvements over prior DRL heuristics, particularly in scalability. The core idea—dynamically focusing on relevant state components—could inspire future work beyond scheduling. However, the incremental architecture and narrow empirical comparisons (omitting state-of-the-art non-DRL methods) limit broader impact. Typos ("dos" L479, "illustrated as follows" L85) should be corrected in the final version.

Summary:
This paper proposes a deep reinforcement learning-based constructive heuristic to solve the (Flexible) Job Shop Scheduling Problem. An instance of the problem is represented as a graph and fed into a Graph Neural Network-based model which outputs a score for each candidate (operation-machine) pair. The model is trained with the REINFORCE algorithm with as baseline a classic Priority Dispatching Rule-based heuristic. The novelty of the paper lies in the update of the state after each action: the graph is updated by removing the operations which have already been executed to focus on the most relevant information, which is the residual operations and remaining times of the ongoing ones. The proposed approach is experimentally shown to outperform RL-based constructive heuristics on classic JSSP and FJSSP benchmarks.

Strengths:
1. Sound and interesting idea of removing irrelevant information from the state 
1. The paper is fairly clear and I appreciated the illustrations Fig 1-3.
1. The model was proposed for the JSSP and easily adapted to the Flexible JSSP
1. The approach outperforms deep RL-based construction heuristics on classic benchmarks

Weaknesses:
1. Limited novelty: the main contribution is the definition of the residual state at each step of the construction process by removing irrelevant operations and resetting the time reference. This seems to me an incremental improvement of the approach L2D [1], which already proposed a similar state graph representation and the use of the Graph Isomorphism Network architecture for the JSSP.
1. The proposed approach seems very specific to (F)JSSPs (state representation, baseline) and it's not clear what could be transferable to DRL heuristics for solving other optimization problems.
1. In the experiments, the presented non-learning-based baselines seem pretty weak: only greedy (see more in Questions)
1. Lots of English typos (I noted a few per page)

[1] C Zhang et al, Learning to Dispatch for Job Shop Scheduling via Deep Reinforcement Learning, Neurips 2020

1. Where dos the attributes of edges (L205) appear in the GNN model (Sec 3.3)?
1.
